1: <header>Influence of plastic mulch and fertilization on the environmental impact of spring cabbage production </header>
Ondrej Ponjican a, Ferenc Kiss b , Zarko Ilin a Boris Adamovica Vladimir Sabados Aleksandar Sedlar Vladimir Visacki a

<list>a University of Novi Sad, Faculty of Agriculture, Trg Dositeja Obradovica 8, 21000 Novi Sad, Serbia 
b University of Novi Sad, Faculty of Technology, Bul. Cara Lazara 1, 21000 Novi Sad, Serbia 
C Agricultural Extension Service "PSS Sombor", Staparski put 35, 25100 Sombor, Serbia</list>

ARTICLEINFO|ABSTRACT Keywords: Plastic mulch Fertilization Environment Life cycle assessment (LCA) Cabbage yield|A three-year field experiment was conducted to determine the influence of plastic mulch (PM) and fertilization methods (FMs) on marketable yield (MY) and environmental impact of spring cabbage production. The trial consisted of two levels of mulching (without and with PM) and nine different FMs, including the control without fertilization (FM1). PM use without fertilization (FM1) increased yields by 35.9 on average, but the positive yield effect of mulching decreased with fertilizer application (FM2-9). Mature cattle manure, composted pig manure and mineral fertilizer increased yields by 21.2 %, 60.6 % and 51.5 %, respectively. Combined fertil- ization gave higher yield up to 73.3% compared to the control (FM1). The environmental impact was evaluated with the ReCiPe 2016 (H) life cycle impact assessment method. The foreground system was modeled mainly using primary data, while background system data were available from the ecoinvent 3.4 generic LCI database. On average across the nine FMs, the application of PM reduced water consumption (23 %), non-carcinogenic toxicity (25 %), terrestrial acidification %), and required land area (11 %), but increased fossil resource scarcity %), global warming %) and fine particulate matter emissions (14%). PM application had positive impacts on ecosystem quality, but regarding human health and resources availability, positive impacts were found only in the control (FM1). Negative environmental impact of PM was the consequence of large amounts of energy used for its production and hazardous emissions through PM disposal. On-site burning of PM under uncontrolled, open burn conditions should be avoided because of negative health impacts. Landfilling and combustion of PM in incinerators with energy recovery could reduce impacts of cabbage production on human health by 16 and 21 %, respectively, as compared to on-site burning. Fertilizer use was environmentally justified as damage indicators were lower on a MY basis compared to the control treatment without fertilization (by 18 %-44%).
2: At global level, cabbage and other brassica vegetables are grown on 2.5 million ha (FAO, 2018a), which makes it the fourth most common vegetable after tomatoes, onion and watermelon (Durovka, 2008). The region of Vojvodina, as well as Serbia, has favorable conditions for growing high quality cabbage for fresh use and industrial production. The cabbage production per capita is 35 kg, which is 17 kg more than the European average of 18 kg. This amount is sufficient for domestic needs and one part is exported (Cervenski and Medic-Pap, 2018). If properly applied, mulching and fertilization are field operations that

have the potential to significantly increase yields of cultivated crops (Easmin et al., 2009; Guo etal., 2011; Kosterna, 2014; Nurhidayati et al., 2016). For agronomic and environmental reasons, it is important to apply optimal types and amounts of fertilizers with suitable application techniques, and test the effects of using new fertilization methods on the yield and quality of the product (Farneselli et al., 2018). In the temperate zones, up to 50 % of the used nitrogen is absorbed by plants, but on a global level, only 30-35 % of nitrogen and around 45 % of phosphorus are absorbed and the rest may contaminate the environment (Guo et al., 2011). In the case of organic fertilizers there is an additional problem since the nutrient release is slow and often difficult to predict
depending on the composition of organic matter, and soil and weather conditions (De Neve, 2017).

There are numerous positive effects of mulching: reduction in water consumption and conservation of soil moisture (Jabran et al., 2015; Kader et al., 2019), reduction in infiltration rate, run-offs and soil erosion (Zhang et al., 2013), reduction in weed growth and pest control (Bucki and Siwek, 2019), improved plant growth and development, increased yield (Gordon et al., 2010; Ma et al., 2018; Yimer, 2020), earlier harvest (Tiwari et al., 2003), reduced fertilizer leaching (Guo et al., 2011, 2019), addition of organic matter (Sarolia and Bhardwaj, 2012) and stimulation of soil micro-flora (Bandopadhyay et al., 2018). Mulching raises soil temperature (Ponjican et al., 2011; Zhang et al., 2015; Domagala-Swiatkiewicz and Siwek, 2018; Chen et al., 2019) which implies faster decomposition of soil organic carbon (SOC) (Trumbore et al., 1996). However, plastic film mulching also enhances the production of above- below-ground biomass (Zhang et al., 2006, 2017) and several studies have concluded that in arid and semi-arid regions increased SOC mineralization under PM is offset by increased SOC addition to soil (Liu et al., 2014; Luo et al., 2015; Wang et al., 2016). In addition, PM is hugely popular in developing countries because PM tends to increase yield and advance maturity (Gordon et al., 2010), which has a positive impact on the revenues; thus, promoting development and progress of social community in rural areas (Mo et al., 2016). PM use is expected to increase steadily in all areas of agricultural production (Sharma et al., 2011; Jabran et al., 2015; Liu et al., 2015; Li et al., 2017; Alliaume et al., 2017) and area under mulching was pro- jected to have grown worldwide by 5.7% annually until 2019 (Kasirajan and Ngouajio, 2012). Between 60 and 80 % of the world mulched-surface is located in China (Espi et al., 2006; Yang et al., 2015) and plastic mulch has become the fourth most demanded agricultural input, just after mineral fertilizer, seed, and pesticide (Yang et al., 2015).

Environmental pollution caused by using and disposing the plastic film is one of the most significant issues that limit its use (Kasirajan and Ngouajio, 2012; Steinmetz et al., 2016). Incorporation of plastic poly- ethylene film in the soil is an unacceptable manner of disposal as it significantly changes the physical properties of soil, prevents the root system from growing steadily, and reduces the yield of subsequently grown crops (Changrong et al., 2014). An equally unacceptable disposal of already used plastic film is on-site burning under uncontrolled, open burn conditions, although it has often been the most common way of agricultural plastic waste disposal (Lawrence, 2007). Landfilling is accepted as a proper way of disposal (Garthe, 2004), but many landfill operators reject plastic mulch film as unsuitable landfill material due to its level of contamination, and because of the fact that it either does not degrade or degrades at a very slow rate and prevents decomposition of other material (Narayan, 2001). Furthermore, landfill disposal requires a large amount of space and conventional landfills were identified as a major source of greenhouse gases (Kumar et al., 2004). Incineration of plastic mulch reduces the need for landfill disposal and in combination with energy recovery it can produce heat and electricity (Lamont, 2005), but greenhouse gases and hazardous air pollutants are also released during the process. Polyethylene mulch recycling can also seem as an option but the problem is too many pesticides, fertilizers, soil and plant residues, which in total mass range up to 40-50 % (Kasirajan and Ngouajio, 2012; Steinmetz et al., 2016). Recycling of plastic film that contains more than of different impurities has long been recognized as a problem from a technological aspect (Clarke, 1996). Furthermore, collection of plastic films requires additional engagement of human labor (Parish et al., 2000; Ponjican et al., 2015) and transportation of loose materials represents additional problems and costs (Moore and Wszelaki, 2016).

Considering the fact that each of these plastic mulch disposal methods has huge impact on the environment, extensive research was conducted with the aim of finding ecologically acceptable photo and biodegradable plastic films (PHA, PCL etc. synthetic- and natural-based polymers films) (Yang et al., 2015; Touchaleaume et al., 2018).

Macroalgae can be used as sustainable alternatives for large-scale pro- duction of biopolymers (Ghosh et al., 2019). In addition to that, another alternative can be the colonies of microorganisms capable of decom- posing the existing plastic material (Jeszeová et al., 2018), but without major commercial use up to now. However the results of life cycle and environmental cycle assessment of biodegradable plastic in agriculture are as well controversial (Razza and Cerutti, 2017).

The impact of plastic mulch and fertilization on the yield of not only cabbage but also other important crops was determined thoroughly in previous research (Memon et al., 2017; Kundu et al., 2019). Much less attention is given for the assessment of environmental impacts of different fertilization methods and mulching practices. In fact, most of the previous environmental studies focused on the assessment of average production technologies (i Canals et al., 2008; Stoessel et al., 2012; Perrin et al., 2014), mostly relying on secondary and aggregated data that are not appropriate to single out the potential environmental consequences of varying the amount of a single input or process vari- able. After extensive literature review, we were not able to identify any comprehensive and detailed analyses of the effects of plastic film mulching and different fertilization practices on the environmental impact of spring cabbage production. In this study, environmental assessment of cabbage production is based on data collected or derived from field experiment and the application of the life cycle assessment (LCA) method which is, according to the European Commission (COM (2003;302), the best framework for assessing the potential environ- mental impacts of products currently available. The specific goal of this research was twofold: firstly, to determine the effects of mulching and different fertilization methods on yields; and secondly, to investigate the environmental justification of using plastic mulch (PM) in function of different fertilization methods (FMs) and PM disposal options.

There are numerous positive effects of mulching: reduction in water consumption and conservation of soil moisture (Jabran et al., 2015; Kader et al., 2019), reduction in infiltration rate, run-offs and soil erosion (Zhang et al., 2013), reduction in weed growth and pest control (Bucki and Siwek, 2019), improved plant growth and development, increased yield (Gordon et al., 2010; Ma et al., 2018; Yimer, 2020), earlier harvest (Tiwari et al., 2003), reduced fertilizer leaching (Guo et al., 2011, 2019), addition of organic matter (Sarolia and Bhardwaj, 2012) and stimulation of soil micro-flora (Bandopadhyay et al., 2018). Mulching raises soil temperature (Ponjican et al., 2011; Zhang et al., 2015; Domagala-Swiatkiewicz and Siwek, 2018; Chen et al., 2019) which implies faster decomposition of soil organic carbon (SOC) (Trumbore et al., 1996). However, plastic film mulching also enhances the production of above- below-ground biomass (Zhang et al., 2006, 2017) and several studies have concluded that in arid and semi-arid regions increased SOC mineralization under PM is offset by increased SOC addition to soil (Liu et al., 2014; Luo et al., 2015; Wang et al., 2016). In addition, PM is hugely popular in developing countries because PM tends to increase yield and advance maturity (Gordon et al., 2010), which has a positive impact on the revenues; thus, promoting development and progress of social community in rural areas (Mo et al., 2016). PM use is expected to increase steadily in all areas of agricultural production (Sharma et al., 2011; Jabran et al., 2015; Liu et al., 2015; Li et al., 2017; Alliaume et al., 2017) and area under mulching was pro- jected to have grown worldwide by 5.7% annually until 2019 (Kasirajan and Ngouajio, 2012). Between 60 and 80 % of the world mulched-surface is located in China (Espi et al., 2006; Yang et al., 2015) and plastic mulch has become the fourth most demanded agricultural input, just after mineral fertilizer, seed, and pesticide (Yang et al., 2015).

Environmental pollution caused by using and disposing the plastic film is one of the most significant issues that limit its use (Kasirajan and Ngouajio, 2012; Steinmetz et al., 2016). Incorporation of plastic poly- ethylene film in the soil is an unacceptable manner of disposal as it significantly changes the physical properties of soil, prevents the root system from growing steadily, and reduces the yield of subsequently grown crops (Changrong et al., 2014). An equally unacceptable disposal of already used plastic film is on-site burning under uncontrolled, open burn conditions, although it has often been the most common way of agricultural plastic waste disposal (Lawrence, 2007). Landfilling is accepted as a proper way of disposal (Garthe, 2004), but many landfill operators reject plastic mulch film as unsuitable landfill material due to its level of contamination, and because of the fact that it either does not degrade or degrades at a very slow rate and prevents decomposition of other material (Narayan, 2001). Furthermore, landfill disposal requires a large amount of space and conventional landfills were identified as a major source of greenhouse gases (Kumar et al., 2004). Incineration of plastic mulch reduces the need for landfill disposal and in combination with energy recovery it can produce heat and electricity (Lamont, 2005), but greenhouse gases and hazardous air pollutants are also released during the process. Polyethylene mulch recycling can also seem as an option but the problem is too many pesticides, fertilizers, soil and plant residues, which in total mass range up to 40-50 % (Kasirajan and Ngouajio, 2012; Steinmetz et al., 2016). Recycling of plastic film that contains more than of different impurities has long been recognized as a problem from a technological aspect (Clarke, 1996). Furthermore, collection of plastic films requires additional engagement of human labor (Parish et al., 2000; Ponjican et al., 2015) and transportation of loose materials represents additional problems and costs (Moore and Wszelaki, 2016).

Considering the fact that each of these plastic mulch disposal methods has huge impact on the environment, extensive research was conducted with the aim of finding ecologically acceptable photo and biodegradable plastic films (PHA, PCL etc. synthetic- and natural-based polymers films) (Yang et al., 2015; Touchaleaume et al., 2018).

Macroalgae can be used as sustainable alternatives for large-scale pro- duction of biopolymers (Ghosh et al., 2019). In addition to that, another alternative can be the colonies of microorganisms capable of decom- posing the existing plastic material (Jeszeová et al., 2018), but without major commercial use up to now. However the results of life cycle and environmental cycle assessment of biodegradable plastic in agriculture are as well controversial (Razza and Cerutti, 2017).

The impact of plastic mulch and fertilization on the yield of not only cabbage but also other important crops was determined thoroughly in previous research (Memon et al., 2017; Kundu et al., 2019). Much less attention is given for the assessment of environmental impacts of different fertilization methods and mulching practices. In fact, most of the previous environmental studies focused on the assessment of average production technologies (i Canals et al., 2008; Stoessel et al., 2012; Perrin et al., 2014), mostly relying on secondary and aggregated data that are not appropriate to single out the potential environmental consequences of varying the amount of a single input or process vari- able. After extensive literature review, we were not able to identify any comprehensive and detailed analyses of the effects of plastic film mulching and different fertilization practices on the environmental impact of spring cabbage production. In this study, environmental assessment of cabbage production is based on data collected or derived from field experiment and the application of the life cycle assessment (LCA) method which is, according to the European Commission (COM (2003;302), the best framework for assessing the potential environ- mental impacts of products currently available. The specific goal of this research was twofold: firstly, to determine the effects of mulching and different fertilization methods on yields; and secondly, to investigate the environmental justification of using plastic mulch (PM) in function of different fertilization methods (FMs) and PM disposal options.
7: At global level, cabbage and other brassica vegetables are grown on 2.5 million ha (FAO, 2018a), which makes it the fourth most common vegetable after tomatoes, onion and watermelon (Durovka, 2008). The region of Vojvodina, as well as Serbia, has favorable conditions for growing high quality cabbage for fresh use and industrial production. The cabbage production per capita is 35 kg, which is 17 kg more than the European average of 18 kg. This amount is sufficient for domestic needs and one part is exported (Cervenski and Medic-Pap, 2018). If properly applied, mulching and fertilization are field operations that

have the potential to significantly increase yields of cultivated crops (Easmin et al., 2009; Guo etal., 2011; Kosterna, 2014; Nurhidayati et al., 2016). For agronomic and environmental reasons, it is important to apply optimal types and amounts of fertilizers with suitable application techniques, and test the effects of using new fertilization methods on the yield and quality of the product (Farneselli et al., 2018). In the temperate zones, up to 50 % of the used nitrogen is absorbed by plants, but on a global level, only 30-35 % of nitrogen and around 45 % of phosphorus are absorbed and the rest may contaminate the environment (Guo et al., 2011). In the case of organic fertilizers there is an additional problem since the nutrient release is slow and often difficult to predict
depending on the composition of organic matter, and soil and weather conditions (De Neve, 2017).

8: A four-year long field experiment was carried out between 2010 and 2013 for the purpose of determining the impact of plastic mulch (PM) and fertilization methods (FMs) on yields and environmental impact of spring cabbage. In 2011, however, the crops were greatly affected by the cabbage fly (Delia radicum) SO this year was not taken into consideration in order to minimize errors arising from non-representative data. In this four-year cycle, the preceding crop of spring cabbage was pepper (Capsicum annuum). The field experiment was carried out at the exper- imental field (45° 75' 13" N and 19° 13' 52" E) of the Agricultural Extension Service, Sombor in Vojvodina, Serbia. The soil type was a Chernozem (FAO, 2014) with a clay loam texture (Supplementary ma- terial, SM 1.1). Meteorological data (SM 1.2) for the whole period of the experiment was collected from the automatic weather station located near the experimental field operated by the Prognostic and Reporting Service for Plant Protection of Vojvodina Province. The average number of days after transplanting the seedlings (DAT) to the harvesting was 70.7 days.
9: Cabbage seedlings were transplanted with 40 cm distance within rows and 60 cm between rows, thus reaching a density of 4.2 plants The size of the plot was 6 m² and there were 25 plants on a single plot per one repetition. In 2010, the experiment was conducted with four replications, and in 2012 and 2013 there were three replications. A split- plot design was used. The main plot treatment was soil mulching without (w/o) or with (w/) non-degradable low-density polyethylene (LDPE) film (detailed properties are provided later in the text), herein- after: plastic mulch (PM). The sub-plot treatment comprised nine levels of fertilization (different type and rates of fertilizer), including the control treatment without fertilization (Table 1), hereinafter:
10: Experimental design with two levels of mulching (with and without PM) and nine levels of fertilization methods (FMs).

note w/o represents without and w/ represents with PM.
11: A complex mineral fertilizer N-P2O5-K2O with formulation of 11-11-21 + microelements applied in rates of 300 and 500 kg ha was used as a mineral fertilizer (MF). Two types of manure fertilizers were used: mature cattle manure (MCM) and composted pig manure (CPM), both in fertilization rate of 20,000 kg ha-1. The amount of nutrients removed from soil with one ton of cabbage head has been given as 5.0 kg N, 1.5 kg P and 5.0 kg K (Lazió et al., 2001). Types of fertilization with manure and mineral fertilizer rates were selected based on the results of chemical analysis of soil and fertilizers (SM 1.3; Table S3 and S4) and expert recommendations. Different fertilization methods and rates were used in order to emphasize the impact of certain fertilizers on the cab- bage yield.
12: <header>2.3. Agrochemical analysis of soil and manure </header>

Agrochemical analysis of soil was conducted annually at depths of 0-30 cm and 30-60 cm prior to the commencement of the experiment (Table 2). The experiment was set up on alkaline soil with medium to high carbonate content, medium content of total nitrogen and high content of mineral nitrogen, medium to poor humus and readily avail- able potassium content, and medium to very poor content of readily available phosphorus (Bogdanovic et al., 2014). Around 90 % of the total nitrogen in manure was in the form of organic nitrogen (Table 3). With respect to MCM, CPM had higher content of N nutrients, P2O5 and K2O (by 43 %, 71 % and 1.2 %, respectively) and higher dry matter content (by 22 %). The agrochemical compositions of soil and manure were determined with standard methods (SM 1.3).
13: <header>2.4. Field operations in spring cabbage production </header>

An overview of field operations associated with spring cabbage production depending on FMs and whether PM is applied is given in Table 4. In the experiment, PM and drip tape laying and transplanting were done manually, but in the analyses these were modeled as mech- anized field operations based on common practice in many countries worldwide (Ponjican et al., 2015). The production technology analyzed in this study, which involves manual hoeing, harvesting and plastic mulch and drip tape collecting, while other field operations were con- ducted with machines, represents the typical cabbage production prac- tice in the region (Cervenski and Medic-Pap, 2018).
Technical characteristics of the agricultural machinery and

equipment are provided in the supporting material, as well as the measured or calculated field capacity and fuel consumption (SM 1.4; Table S5). The same type and application rate of pesticides were applied in each of the treatments (SM 1.5; Table S6 and S7). Soil mulching was performed by applying non-degradable black LDPE film (density 920 kg thickness 20 um, width 80 cm), with two rows of cabbage being planted on one mulch strip. The total mass of applied PM was 122.67 kg ha-1 and approximately the same mass was used for mulching other crops (Ponjican et al., 2015). The drip irrigation method was applied with one dripper liner being installed along every row of planted cab- bage. Cabbage was irrigated 7.33 and 6.33 times, on average, depending on applied PM (w/o and w/), with the rate of 26.6 mm per irrigation (SI 1.5; Table S7). Schedule of irrigation was conducted based on soil moisture content, metered by tensiometer (IRROMETER SR12, vacuum gauge in range 0-100 kPa, manual reading, with ceramic extension placed at depth of 30 cm) and kept above 80 % of soil water potential (Nenadov, 1981).

Technical characteristics of the agricultural machinery and

equipment are provided in the supporting material, as well as the measured or calculated field capacity and fuel consumption (SM 1.4; Table S5). 15: Life cycle assessment (LCA) was conducted following the principles of attributional LCA (Sonnemann and Vigon, 2011) and the cut-off modeling approach (Steubing et al., 2016). Primary data collected or derived from the field experiments were used to describe the processes and flows associated with the foreground system, while the background system was modeled with generic or average life cycle inventory (LCI) datasets largely available from the ecoinvent 3.4. LCI database (Steubing et al., 2016). The newly developed LCI datasets or the modified ecoin- vent processes are briefly described in the following subchapters, while details on the modeling assumptions, calculation procedures and data sources are presented in the supplementary material (SM 1.6). The life cycle environmental impacts were estimated using the ReCiPe 2016 life cycle impact assessment (LCIA) method (Huijbregts et al., 2017) following the hierarchist cultural perspective. The ReCiPe method ex- presses LCIA results through the results of 18 midpoint (Table 5) and 22 endpoint indicators. Endpoint category indicators are organized in three damage categories, allowing to quantify the damage caused to human health, ecosystem quality and resource availability. Ecoinvent 3.4 LCI database and the ReCiPe 2016 LCIA method are both integrated into the OpenLCA 1.7 LCA software (Di Noi et al., 2017) which was used to calculate the results. If not stated otherwise, LCA results refer to one functional unit which was defined as 1000 kg of MY (cabbage heads, fresh weight).
16: The amount of machinery (in kg ha-1 necessary for a specific field operation was calculated based on the typical weight, anticipated life time and the measured operation time of specific agricultural machinery (SM 1.6.1; Table S8) using the procedure recommended by Nemecek et al. (2007).

Seedlings were produced in unheated greenhouse in compressed square blocks (3 X 3 X 3 cm) using peat moss as a growing medium. Around 2.67 m³ of peat moss is needed to produce 41 thousand seedlings required for establishing cabbage production per one hectare. Primary data on the size of greenhouse, transport modes and the consumption of materials and utilities were collected from the seedling producer, while the upstream processes were modeled based on ecoinvent data (SM 1.6.2; Table S9).

manure were estimated based on the latest EMEP/EAA guideline (Hutchings et al., 2016) which provides emission factors for various nitrogenous fertilizers and manure. The same simplified approach was used to estimate the ammonia (NH3) emissions from mineral fertilizers. NH3 emissions from manure were estimated with the method used by Aguirre-Villegas and Larson (2017) which considers time of incorpora- tion as a potentially relevant variable. Nitrogen leaching in form of NO3 was estimated from the surplus of N using the function recommended by Roy et al. (2003). Compared to simplified approaches based on general leaching factors (De Klein et al., 2006) this method considers some local data, such as soil condition, annual precipitation level, root depth, which allows case-specific estimations. N2O emissions from the appli- cation of ammonium nitrate fertilizer and manure were estimated using the IPCC 2006 Tier 1 method (De Klein et al., 2006). The annual leaching loss of phosphate to ground water in the investigated region is on average 0.25 kg PO4 ha year (Ubavic and Bogdanovic, 2001) regardless of the applied amount of fertilizers and manure. Phosphate run-off to surface water was calculated with the equation assuming the average emission factors for arable land corrected with the presented factors for mineral fertilizers and manure (Nemecek et al., 2014). Phosphorous emission through erosion to surface water was calculated based on the equation recommended by Nemecek et al. (2014) and using local or country-specific data on the quantity of eroded soil that reaches the rivers and the average phosphorus content in the eroded soil. The estimated quantities of N and P emissions associated with cabbage production are presented in the supplementary material (SM 1.6.3. and 1.6.4; Tables S10 and S11).
The ecoinvent 3.4 database provides LCI data of several pesticides. For those active ingredients that are not listed in the ecoinvent database,

the respective impacts were approximated based on the average impacts of the substance group to which the active ingredient belongs. The ecoinvent approach applied for pesticides assumes that 100 % of the applied pesticide is emitted into the soil (Nemecek and Schnetzer, 2011), which is clearly an oversimplification. Therefore, the fate of the pesticides and their distribution in various environmental compartments were modeled with the PestLCI 2.0 software (Dijkman et al., 2012) considering local meteorological and soil data, case-specific application methods and plant maturity (SM 1.6.5; Fig. S2; Table S12). The distri- bution of copper-hydroxide, an inorganic pesticide, is inventoried as heavy metal emission (e.g. copper ion) (Christel et al., 2014) into spe- cific environmental compartments (Peña Valbuena, 2018) (SM 1.6.5; Table S13).

The heavy metal balance was calculated as the difference between heavy metals added to the soil with fertilizers and manure and heavy metals removed with biomass. Heavy metal content of the applied manure was experimentally measured from the samples collected during the field experiment (SM 1.6.6; Table S14), while literature data were used to estimate the heavy metal content of mineral fertilizers (Nemecek et al., 2014) and cabbage (Petrovié-Gegic et al., 2007; Osmanovió and Huseinovié, 2015; Stanció et al., 2016) (SM 1.6.6; Table S15). Crop residues left on the field were not considered in the balance, since they did not leave the system. All heavy metal emissions were considered as emissions into agricultural soil, which is the default modeling approach in the ecoinvent LCI database. If the uptake of heavy metals exceeds the inputs, a negative balance will result (SM 1.6.6; Table S16).

Plastic mulch and drip tapes were made from LDPE, while the irri- gation pipes and equipment were mainly made (92 %) from high-density polyethylene (HDPE) and the rest was cast iron. Plastic mulch and drip

Notes: "The damage to human health is measured in DALYs (disability adjusted loss of life years). One DALY can be thought of as one lost year of "healthy" life due to a disease or accident; The unit for ecosystem quality is the local species loss integrated over time; 'The damage to resource availability is measured in USD, which represents the extra costs involved for future mineral and fossil resource extraction due to the decrease in the availability of the total resource stock. Detailed explanation of the midpoint and endpoint indicators, charac- terization factors and units is available in Huijbregts et al. (2017).

tapes are replaced each year (Ponjican et al., 2015, 2017), while irri- gation pipes have an anticipated life span of 15 years. According to the common practice in Vojvodina, it was assumed that 75 of PM is burnt on-site under uncontrolled, open burn conditions, while the rest is transported to sanitary landfills (Ponjican et al., 2015). Wrobel and Reinhardt (2003) reported minimal and maximal emission factors for the range of polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons (PAHs) and volatile organic compounds (VOCs) associated with open field burning of agri- cultural plastic films. Other relevant emission factors (CO2, NO and N20) were taken from Doka (2015), which reflected the typical emis- sions due to the combustion of waste polyethylene in a municipal solid waste incinerator (MSWI). Average emission factors were used in the default scenario, but the sensitivity of LCA results on the chosen emis- sion factors was also tested by considering the lowest and highest emission factors of PAHs and VOCs 1.6.7; Table S18) as reported by Wrobel and Reinhardt (2003). Regarding the end-of-life (EOL) of drip tapes, it was assumed that 75 % would be treated as PM, while the rest would be recycled (Ponjican et al., 2017). EoL of irrigation pipes and equipment was modeled using default ecoinvent data (Tables S21-S22).

The manure was assumed to be applied in a four-year cycle which is the recommended practice in Serbia (Manojlovié, 2008). Therefore, a part of the impacts associated with manure was allocated to subsequent crops in the crop rotation. Impacts associated with the production and storage of manure were allocated to animal production which means that manure is available burden-free for cabbage production, and only the impacts associated with the transport, distribution and subsequent emissions to the environment were allocated between crops in the crop rotation. This approach is in line with the cut-off modeling principle and FAO guidelines for the assessment of animal supply chain (FAO, 2018b). Van Zeijts et al. (1999) suggested allocating the burdens associated with

manure application according to the land-use share of each crop in the rotation (so called time-based allocation); whereas Martínez-Blanco et al. (2014) identified N mineralization rates and N uptake as the most promising approach. In this study, the N-based allocation was chosen as the default approach, but the influence of time-based allocation on LCA results was tested with sensitivity analysis.

The whole dataset was statistically analyzed by factorial ANOVA. The statistical significance of differences was determined by the Dun- can's multiple range test. In order to determine the influence of PM and FMs on MY, multiple linear regression was applied. To determine the strength of the relationship between individual factors and MY, partial correlation coefficients were calculated (Kleinbaum et al., 1988). All listed tests were performed at the significance level of a = 0.05. The analyses were carried out using the Statistica 13 software (TIBCO Soft- ware Inc.).

manure were estimated based on the latest EMEP/EAA guideline (Hutchings et al., 2016) which provides emission factors for various nitrogenous fertilizers and manure. The same simplified approach was used to estimate the ammonia (NH3) emissions from mineral fertilizers. NH3 emissions from manure were estimated with the method used by Aguirre-Villegas and Larson (2017) which considers time of incorpora- tion as a potentially relevant variable. Nitrogen leaching in form of NO3 was estimated from the surplus of N using the function recommended by Roy et al. (2003). Compared to simplified approaches based on general leaching factors (De Klein et al., 2006) this method considers some local data, such as soil condition, annual precipitation level, root depth, which allows case-specific estimations. N2O emissions from the appli- cation of ammonium nitrate fertilizer and manure were estimated using the IPCC 2006 Tier 1 method (De Klein et al., 2006). The annual leaching loss of phosphate to ground water in the investigated region is on average 0.25 kg PO4 ha year (Ubavic and Bogdanovic, 2001) regardless of the applied amount of fertilizers and manure. Phosphate run-off to surface water was calculated with the equation assuming the average emission factors for arable land corrected with the presented factors for mineral fertilizers and manure (Nemecek et al., 2014). Phosphorous emission through erosion to surface water was calculated based on the equation recommended by Nemecek et al. (2014) and using local or country-specific data on the quantity of eroded soil that reaches the rivers and the average phosphorus content in the eroded soil. The estimated quantities of N and P emissions associated with cabbage production are presented in the supplementary material (SM 1.6.3. and 1.6.4; Tables S10 and S11).
The ecoinvent 3.4 database provides LCI data of several pesticides. For those active ingredients that are not listed in the ecoinvent database,

the respective impacts were approximated based on the average impacts of the substance group to which the active ingredient belongs. The ecoinvent approach applied for pesticides assumes that 100 % of the applied pesticide is emitted into the soil (Nemecek and Schnetzer, 2011), which is clearly an oversimplification. Therefore, the fate of the pesticides and their distribution in various environmental compartments were modeled with the PestLCI 2.0 software (Dijkman et al., 2012) considering local meteorological and soil data, case-specific application methods and plant maturity (SM 1.6.5; Fig. S2; Table S12). The distri- bution of copper-hydroxide, an inorganic pesticide, is inventoried as heavy metal emission (e.g. copper ion) (Christel et al., 2014) into spe- cific environmental compartments (Peña Valbuena, 2018) (SM 1.6.5; Table S13).

The heavy metal balance was calculated as the difference between heavy metals added to the soil with fertilizers and manure and heavy metals removed with biomass. Heavy metal content of the applied manure was experimentally measured from the samples collected during the field experiment (SM 1.6.6; Table S14), while literature data were used to estimate the heavy metal content of mineral fertilizers (Nemecek et al., 2014) and cabbage (Petrovié-Gegic et al., 2007; Osmanovió and Huseinovié, 2015; Stanció et al., 2016) (SM 1.6.6; Table S15). Crop residues left on the field were not considered in the balance, since they did not leave the system. All heavy metal emissions were considered as emissions into agricultural soil, which is the default modeling approach in the ecoinvent LCI database. If the uptake of heavy metals exceeds the inputs, a negative balance will result (SM 1.6.6; Table S16).

Plastic mulch and drip tapes were made from LDPE, while the irri- gation pipes and equipment were mainly made (92 %) from high-density polyethylene (HDPE) and the rest was cast iron. Plastic mulch and drip

Name|Abbreviation|Unit Midpoint impact categories|Midpoint impact categories|Midpoint impact categories Ozone formation, Human health|OHH|kg NO eq Fine particulate matter formation|PMT|kg PMT eq Marine ecotoxicity|MET|kg 1,4-DCB eq Terrestrial ecotoxicity|TET|kg 1,4-DCB eq Water consumption|WD|m³ Human carcinogenic toxicity|HTc|kg 1,4-DCB eq Marine eutrophication|MEU|kg N eq Human non-carcinogenic toxicity|HTnc|kg 1,4-DCB eq Stratospheric ozone depletion|OST|kg CFC 11 eq Land use|LU|m² annual crop eq Terrestrial acidification|TAC|kg SO2 eq Ozone formation, Terrestrial ecosystems|OTE|kg NO eq Global warming|GW|kg CO2 eq Freshwater eutrophication|WEU|kg eq Mineral resource scarcity|MD|kg Cu eq Fossil resource scarcity|FD|kg oil eq Freshwater ecotoxicity|WET|kg 1,4-DCB eq Ionizing radiation|I|kBq Co-60 eq Endpoint impact categories|| Damage to human health|HH|DALY a Damage to ecosystem quality|ED|species X yr b Damage to resource availability|RA|USD C

Notes: "The damage to human health is measured in DALYs (disability adjusted loss of life years). One DALY can be thought of as one lost year of "healthy" life due to a disease or accident; The unit for ecosystem quality is the local species loss integrated over time; 'The damage to resource availability is measured in USD, which represents the extra costs involved for future mineral and fossil resource extraction due to the decrease in the availability of the total resource stock. Detailed explanation of the midpoint and endpoint indicators, charac- terization factors and units is available in Huijbregts et al. (2017).

tapes are replaced each year (Ponjican et al., 2015, 2017), while irri- gation pipes have an anticipated life span of 15 years. According to the common practice in Vojvodina, it was assumed that 75 of PM is burnt on-site under uncontrolled, open burn conditions, while the rest is transported to sanitary landfills (Ponjican et al., 2015). Wrobel and Reinhardt (2003) reported minimal and maximal emission factors for the range of polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons (PAHs) and volatile organic compounds (VOCs) associated with open field burning of agri- cultural plastic films. Other relevant emission factors (CO2, NO and N20) were taken from Doka (2015), which reflected the typical emis- sions due to the combustion of waste polyethylene in a municipal solid waste incinerator (MSWI). Average emission factors were used in the default scenario, but the sensitivity of LCA results on the chosen emis- sion factors was also tested by considering the lowest and highest emission factors of PAHs and VOCs 1.6.7; Table S18) as reported by Wrobel and Reinhardt (2003). Regarding the end-of-life (EOL) of drip tapes, it was assumed that 75 % would be treated as PM, while the rest would be recycled (Ponjican et al., 2017). EoL of irrigation pipes and equipment was modeled using default ecoinvent data (Tables S21-S22).

The manure was assumed to be applied in a four-year cycle which is the recommended practice in Serbia (Manojlovié, 2008). Therefore, a part of the impacts associated with manure was allocated to subsequent crops in the crop rotation. Impacts associated with the production and storage of manure were allocated to animal production which means that manure is available burden-free for cabbage production, and only the impacts associated with the transport, distribution and subsequent emissions to the environment were allocated between crops in the crop rotation. This approach is in line with the cut-off modeling principle and FAO guidelines for the assessment of animal supply chain (FAO, 2018b). Van Zeijts et al. (1999) suggested allocating the burdens associated with

manure application according to the land-use share of each crop in the rotation (so called time-based allocation); whereas Martínez-Blanco et al. (2014) identified N mineralization rates and N uptake as the most promising approach. In this study, the N-based allocation was chosen as the default approach, but the influence of time-based allocation on LCA results was tested with sensitivity analysis.

The whole dataset was statistically analyzed by factorial ANOVA. The statistical significance of differences was determined by the Dun- can's multiple range test. In order to determine the influence of PM and FMs on MY, multiple linear regression was applied. To determine the strength of the relationship between individual factors and MY, partial correlation coefficients were calculated (Kleinbaum et al., 1988). All listed tests were performed at the significance level of a = 0.05. The analyses were carried out using the Statistica 13 software (TIBCO Soft- ware Inc.).

manure were estimated based on the latest EMEP/EAA guideline (Hutchings et al., 2016) which provides emission factors for various nitrogenous fertilizers and manure. The same simplified approach was used to estimate the ammonia (NH3) emissions from mineral fertilizers. NH3 emissions from manure were estimated with the method used by Aguirre-Villegas and Larson (2017) which considers time of incorpora- tion as a potentially relevant variable. Nitrogen leaching in form of NO3 was estimated from the surplus of N using the function recommended by Roy et al. (2003). Compared to simplified approaches based on general leaching factors (De Klein et al., 2006) this method considers some local data, such as soil condition, annual precipitation level, root depth, which allows case-specific estimations. N2O emissions from the appli- cation of ammonium nitrate fertilizer and manure were estimated using the IPCC 2006 Tier 1 method (De Klein et al., 2006). The annual leaching loss of phosphate to ground water in the investigated region is on average 0.25 kg PO4 ha year (Ubavic and Bogdanovic, 2001) regardless of the applied amount of fertilizers and manure. Phosphate run-off to surface water was calculated with the equation assuming the average emission factors for arable land corrected with the presented factors for mineral fertilizers and manure (Nemecek et al., 2014). Phosphorous emission through erosion to surface water was calculated based on the equation recommended by Nemecek et al. (2014) and using local or country-specific data on the quantity of eroded soil that reaches the rivers and the average phosphorus content in the eroded soil. The estimated quantities of N and P emissions associated with cabbage production are presented in the supplementary material (SM 1.6.3. and 1.6.4; Tables S10 and S11).
The ecoinvent 3.4 database provides LCI data of several pesticides. For those active ingredients that are not listed in the ecoinvent database,

the respective impacts were approximated based on the average impacts of the substance group to which the active ingredient belongs. The ecoinvent approach applied for pesticides assumes that 100 % of the applied pesticide is emitted into the soil (Nemecek and Schnetzer, 2011), which is clearly an oversimplification. Therefore, the fate of the pesticides and their distribution in various environmental compartments were modeled with the PestLCI 2.0 software (Dijkman et al., 2012) considering local meteorological and soil data, case-specific application methods and plant maturity (SM 1.6.5; Fig. S2; Table S12). The distri- bution of copper-hydroxide, an inorganic pesticide, is inventoried as heavy metal emission (e.g. copper ion) (Christel et al., 2014) into spe- cific environmental compartments (Peña Valbuena, 2018) (SM 1.6.5; Table S13).

The heavy metal balance was calculated as the difference between heavy metals added to the soil with fertilizers and manure and heavy metals removed with biomass. Heavy metal content of the applied manure was experimentally measured from the samples collected during the field experiment (SM 1.6.6; Table S14), while literature data were used to estimate the heavy metal content of mineral fertilizers (Nemecek et al., 2014) and cabbage (Petrovié-Gegic et al., 2007; Osmanovió and Huseinovié, 2015; Stanció et al., 2016) (SM 1.6.6; Table S15). Crop residues left on the field were not considered in the balance, since they did not leave the system. All heavy metal emissions were considered as emissions into agricultural soil, which is the default modeling approach in the ecoinvent LCI database. If the uptake of heavy metals exceeds the inputs, a negative balance will result (SM 1.6.6; Table S16).

Heavy metal content of the applied manure was experimentally measured from the samples collected during the field experiment (SM 1.6.6; Table S14), while literature data were used to estimate the heavy metal content of mineral fertilizers (Nemecek et al., 2014) and cabbage (Petrovié-Gegic et al., 2007; Osmanovió and Huseinovié, 2015; Stanció et al., 2016) (SM 1.6.6; Table S15). Crop residues left on the field were not considered in the balance, since they did not leave the system. All heavy metal emissions were considered as emissions into agricultural soil, which is the default modeling approach in the ecoinvent LCI database. If the uptake of heavy metals exceeds the inputs, a negative balance will result (SM 1.6.6; Table S16).

Plastic mulch and drip tapes were made from LDPE, while the irri- gation pipes and equipment were mainly made (92 %) from high-density polyethylene (HDPE) and the rest was cast iron. Plastic mulch and drip
Names, abbreviation and units of the 18 midpoint and the 3 aggregated endpoint categories in the ReCiPe 2016 LCIA method.

Name|Abbreviation|Unit Midpoint impact categories|Midpoint impact categories|Midpoint impact categories Ozone formation, Human health|OHH|kg NO eq Fine particulate matter formation|PMT|kg PMT eq Marine ecotoxicity|MET|kg 1,4-DCB eq Terrestrial ecotoxicity|TET|kg 1,4-DCB eq Water consumption|WD|m³ Human carcinogenic toxicity|HTc|kg 1,4-DCB eq Marine eutrophication|MEU|kg N eq Human non-carcinogenic toxicity|HTnc|kg 1,4-DCB eq Stratospheric ozone depletion|OST|kg CFC 11 eq Land use|LU|m² annual crop eq Terrestrial acidification|TAC|kg SO2 eq Ozone formation, Terrestrial ecosystems|OTE|kg NO eq Global warming|GW|kg CO2 eq Freshwater eutrophication|WEU|kg eq Mineral resource scarcity|MD|kg Cu eq Fossil resource scarcity|FD|kg oil eq Freshwater ecotoxicity|WET|kg 1,4-DCB eq Ionizing radiation|I|kBq Co-60 eq Endpoint impact categories|| Damage to human health|HH|DALY a Damage to ecosystem quality|ED|species X yr b Damage to resource availability|RA|USD C

Notes: "The damage to human health is measured in DALYs (disability adjusted loss of life years). One DALY can be thought of as one lost year of "healthy" life due to a disease or accident; The unit for ecosystem quality is the local species loss integrated over time; 'The damage to resource availability is measured in USD, which represents the extra costs involved for future mineral and fossil resource extraction due to the decrease in the availability of the total resource stock. Detailed explanation of the midpoint and endpoint indicators, charac- terization factors and units is available in Huijbregts et al. (2017).

tapes are replaced each year (Ponjican et al., 2015, 2017), while irri- gation pipes have an anticipated life span of 15 years. According to the common practice in Vojvodina, it was assumed that 75 of PM is burnt on-site under uncontrolled, open burn conditions, while the rest is transported to sanitary landfills (Ponjican et al., 2015). Wrobel and Reinhardt (2003) reported minimal and maximal emission factors for the range of polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons (PAHs) and volatile organic compounds (VOCs) associated with open field burning of agri- cultural plastic films. Other relevant emission factors (CO2, NO and N20) were taken from Doka (2015), which reflected the typical emis- sions due to the combustion of waste polyethylene in a municipal solid waste incinerator (MSWI). Average emission factors were used in the default scenario, but the sensitivity of LCA results on the chosen emis- sion factors was also tested by considering the lowest and highest emission factors of PAHs and VOCs 1.6.7; Table S18) as reported by Wrobel and Reinhardt (2003). Regarding the end-of-life (EOL) of drip tapes, it was assumed that 75 % would be treated as PM, while the rest would be recycled (Ponjican et al., 2017). EoL of irrigation pipes and equipment was modeled using default ecoinvent data (Tables S21-S22).

The manure was assumed to be applied in a four-year cycle which is the recommended practice in Serbia (Manojlovié, 2008). Therefore, a part of the impacts associated with manure was allocated to subsequent crops in the crop rotation. Impacts associated with the production and storage of manure were allocated to animal production which means that manure is available burden-free for cabbage production, and only the impacts associated with the transport, distribution and subsequent emissions to the environment were allocated between crops in the crop rotation. This approach is in line with the cut-off modeling principle and FAO guidelines for the assessment of animal supply chain (FAO, 2018b). Van Zeijts et al. (1999) suggested allocating the burdens associated with

manure application according to the land-use share of each crop in the rotation (so called time-based allocation); whereas Martínez-Blanco et al. (2014) identified N mineralization rates and N uptake as the most promising approach. In this study, the N-based allocation was chosen as the default approach, but the influence of time-based allocation on LCA results was tested with sensitivity analysis.

The whole dataset was statistically analyzed by factorial ANOVA. The statistical significance of differences was determined by the Dun- can's multiple range test. In order to determine the influence of PM and FMs on MY, multiple linear regression was applied. To determine the strength of the relationship between individual factors and MY, partial correlation coefficients were calculated (Kleinbaum et al., 1988). All listed tests were performed at the significance level of a = 0.05. The analyses were carried out using the Statistica 13 software (TIBCO Soft- ware Inc.).

manure were estimated based on the latest EMEP/EAA guideline (Hutchings et al., 2016) which provides emission factors for various nitrogenous fertilizers and manure. The same simplified approach was used to estimate the ammonia (NH3) emissions from mineral fertilizers. NH3 emissions from manure were estimated with the method used by Aguirre-Villegas and Larson (2017) which considers time of incorpora- tion as a potentially relevant variable. Nitrogen leaching in form of NO3 was estimated from the surplus of N using the function recommended by Roy et al. (2003). Compared to simplified approaches based on general leaching factors (De Klein et al., 2006) this method considers some local data, such as soil condition, annual precipitation level, root depth, which allows case-specific estimations. N2O emissions from the appli- cation of ammonium nitrate fertilizer and manure were estimated using the IPCC 2006 Tier 1 method (De Klein et al., 2006). The annual leaching loss of phosphate to ground water in the investigated region is on average 0.25 kg PO4 ha year (Ubavic and Bogdanovic, 2001) regardless of the applied amount of fertilizers and manure. Phosphate run-off to surface water was calculated with the equation assuming the average emission factors for arable land corrected with the presented factors for mineral fertilizers and manure (Nemecek et al., 2014). Phosphorous emission through erosion to surface water was calculated based on the equation recommended by Nemecek et al. (2014) and using local or country-specific data on the quantity of eroded soil that reaches the rivers and the average phosphorus content in the eroded soil. The estimated quantities of N and P emissions associated with cabbage production are presented in the supplementary material (SM 1.6.3. and 1.6.4; Tables S10 and S11).
The ecoinvent 3.4 database provides LCI data of several pesticides. For those active ingredients that are not listed in the ecoinvent database,

the respective impacts were approximated based on the average impacts of the substance group to which the active ingredient belongs. The ecoinvent approach applied for pesticides assumes that 100 % of the applied pesticide is emitted into the soil (Nemecek and Schnetzer, 2011), which is clearly an oversimplification. Therefore, the fate of the pesticides and their distribution in various environmental compartments were modeled with the PestLCI 2.0 software (Dijkman et al., 2012) considering local meteorological and soil data, case-specific application methods and plant maturity (SM 1.6.5; Fig. S2; Table S12). The distri- bution of copper-hydroxide, an inorganic pesticide, is inventoried as heavy metal emission (e.g. copper ion) (Christel et al., 2014) into spe- cific environmental compartments (Peña Valbuena, 2018) (SM 1.6.5; Table S13).

Crop residues left on the field were not considered in the balance, since they did not leave the system. All heavy metal emissions were considered as emissions into agricultural soil, which is the default modeling approach in the ecoinvent LCI database. If the uptake of heavy metals exceeds the inputs, a negative balance will result (SM 1.6.6; Table S16).

The ecoinvent approach applied for pesticides assumes that 100 % of the applied pesticide is emitted into the soil (Nemecek and Schnetzer, 2011), which is clearly an oversimplification. Therefore, the fate of the pesticides and their distribution in various environmental compartments were modeled with the PestLCI 2.0 software (Dijkman et al., 2012) considering local meteorological and soil data, case-specific application methods and plant maturity (SM 1.6.5; Fig. S2; Table S12). The distri- bution of copper-hydroxide, an inorganic pesticide, is inventoried as heavy metal emission (e.g. copper ion) (Christel et al., 2014) into spe- cific environmental compartments (Peña Valbuena, 2018) (SM 1.6.5; Table S13).

The heavy metal balance was calculated as the difference between heavy metals added to the soil with fertilizers and manure and heavy metals removed with biomass. Heavy metal content of the applied manure was experimentally measured from the samples collected during the field experiment (SM 1.6.6; Table S14), while literature data were used to estimate the heavy metal content of mineral fertilizers (Nemecek et al., 2014) and cabbage (Petrovié-Gegic et al., 2007; Osmanovió and Huseinovié, 2015; Stanció et al., 2016) (SM 1.6.6; Table S15). Crop residues left on the field were not considered in the balance, since they did not leave the system. All heavy metal emissions were considered as emissions into agricultural soil, which is the default modeling approach in the ecoinvent LCI database. If the uptake of heavy metals exceeds the inputs, a negative balance will result (SM 1.6.6; Table S16).

Plastic mulch and drip tapes were made from LDPE, while the irri- gation pipes and equipment were mainly made (92 %) from high-density polyethylene (HDPE) and the rest was cast iron. Plastic mulch and drip
Names, abbreviation and units of the 18 midpoint and the 3 aggregated endpoint categories in the ReCiPe 2016 LCIA method.

Name|Abbreviation|Unit Midpoint impact categories|Midpoint impact categories|Midpoint impact categories Ozone formation, Human health|OHH|kg NO eq Fine particulate matter formation|PMT|kg PMT eq Marine ecotoxicity|MET|kg 1,4-DCB eq Terrestrial ecotoxicity|TET|kg 1,4-DCB eq Water consumption|WD|m³ Human carcinogenic toxicity|HTc|kg 1,4-DCB eq Marine eutrophication|MEU|kg N eq Human non-carcinogenic toxicity|HTnc|kg 1,4-DCB eq Stratospheric ozone depletion|OST|kg CFC 11 eq Land use|LU|m² annual crop eq Terrestrial acidification|TAC|kg SO2 eq Ozone formation, Terrestrial ecosystems|OTE|kg NO eq Global warming|GW|kg CO2 eq Freshwater eutrophication|WEU|kg eq Mineral resource scarcity|MD|kg Cu eq Fossil resource scarcity|FD|kg oil eq Freshwater ecotoxicity|WET|kg 1,4-DCB eq Ionizing radiation|I|kBq Co-60 eq Endpoint impact categories|| Damage to human health|HH|DALY a Damage to ecosystem quality|ED|species X yr b Damage to resource availability|RA|USD C

Notes: "The damage to human health is measured in DALYs (disability adjusted loss of life years). One DALY can be thought of as one lost year of "healthy" life due to a disease or accident; The unit for ecosystem quality is the local species loss integrated over time; 'The damage to resource availability is measured in USD, which represents the extra costs involved for future mineral and fossil resource extraction due to the decrease in the availability of the total resource stock. Detailed explanation of the midpoint and endpoint indicators, charac- terization factors and units is available in Huijbregts et al. (2017).

tapes are replaced each year (Ponjican et al., 2015, 2017), while irri- gation pipes have an anticipated life span of 15 years. According to the common practice in Vojvodina, it was assumed that 75 of PM is burnt on-site under uncontrolled, open burn conditions, while the rest is transported to sanitary landfills (Ponjican et al., 2015). Wrobel and Reinhardt (2003) reported minimal and maximal emission factors for the range of polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons (PAHs) and volatile organic compounds (VOCs) associated with open field burning of agri- cultural plastic films. Other relevant emission factors (CO2, NO and N20) were taken from Doka (2015), which reflected the typical emis- sions due to the combustion of waste polyethylene in a municipal solid waste incinerator (MSWI). Average emission factors were used in the default scenario, but the sensitivity of LCA results on the chosen emis- sion factors was also tested by considering the lowest and highest emission factors of PAHs and VOCs 1.6.7; Table S18) as reported by Wrobel and Reinhardt (2003). Regarding the end-of-life (EOL) of drip tapes, it was assumed that 75 % would be treated as PM, while the rest would be recycled (Ponjican et al., 2017). EoL of irrigation pipes and equipment was modeled using default ecoinvent data (Tables S21-S22).

The manure was assumed to be applied in a four-year cycle which is the recommended practice in Serbia (Manojlovié, 2008). Therefore, a part of the impacts associated with manure was allocated to subsequent crops in the crop rotation. Impacts associated with the production and storage of manure were allocated to animal production which means that manure is available burden-free for cabbage production, and only the impacts associated with the transport, distribution and subsequent emissions to the environment were allocated between crops in the crop rotation. This approach is in line with the cut-off modeling principle and FAO guidelines for the assessment of animal supply chain (FAO, 2018b). Van Zeijts et al. (1999) suggested allocating the burdens associated with

manure application according to the land-use share of each crop in the rotation (so called time-based allocation); whereas Martínez-Blanco et al. (2014) identified N mineralization rates and N uptake as the most promising approach. In this study, the N-based allocation was chosen as the default approach, but the influence of time-based allocation on LCA results was tested with sensitivity analysis.

The whole dataset was statistically analyzed by factorial ANOVA. The statistical significance of differences was determined by the Dun- can's multiple range test. In order to determine the influence of PM and FMs on MY, multiple linear regression was applied. To determine the strength of the relationship between individual factors and MY, partial correlation coefficients were calculated (Kleinbaum et al., 1988). All listed tests were performed at the significance level of a = 0.05. The analyses were carried out using the Statistica 13 software (TIBCO Soft- ware Inc.).

manure were estimated based on the latest EMEP/EAA guideline (Hutchings et al., 2016) which provides emission factors for various nitrogenous fertilizers and manure. The same simplified approach was used to estimate the ammonia (NH3) emissions from mineral fertilizers. NH3 emissions from manure were estimated with the method used by Aguirre-Villegas and Larson (2017) which considers time of incorpora- tion as a potentially relevant variable. Nitrogen leaching in form of NO3 was estimated from the surplus of N using the function recommended by Roy et al. (2003). Compared to simplified approaches based on general leaching factors (De Klein et al., 2006) this method considers some local data, such as soil condition, annual precipitation level, root depth, which allows case-specific estimations. N2O emissions from the appli- cation of ammonium nitrate fertilizer and manure were estimated using the IPCC 2006 Tier 1 method (De Klein et al., 2006). The annual leaching loss of phosphate to ground water in the investigated region is on average 0.25 kg PO4 ha year (Ubavic and Bogdanovic, 2001) regardless of the applied amount of fertilizers and manure. Phosphate run-off to surface water was calculated with the equation assuming the average emission factors for arable land corrected with the presented factors for mineral fertilizers and manure (Nemecek et al., 2014). Phosphorous emission through erosion to surface water was calculated based on the equation recommended by Nemecek et al. (2014) and using local or country-specific data on the quantity of eroded soil that reaches the rivers and the average phosphorus content in the eroded soil. The estimated quantities of N and P emissions associated with cabbage production are presented in the supplementary material (SM 1.6.3. and 1.6.4; Tables S10 and S11).
