30: 3.1. Cabbage yield in function of plastic mulch and fertilization methods

The output from cabbage production is its total biological yield (TY), but marketable yield (MY), which represents the weight of the cabbage head, is more relevant, both from economic and LCA perspectives (SM 2.1; Table S25). Differences in MY depending on PM and FMs and their statistical significant differences at the 5 % significance level are shown in Fig. 1.

Depending on the year of investigation, in 2010 and 2012 (Fig. 1a,b), considerably higher average yields of cabbage head (MY) (48.45 and 55.40 t ha were achieved as compared to 2013 when the average yield was only 27.48 t ha- (Fig. 1c).

In 2010 and 2013 (Fig. 1a,c) statistically significant differences in MYs were observed between the treatments w/o and w/ PM, and the application of PM resulted on average yield increase (aggregated results observed from all FMs) of 21.08% and 26.14%, respectively. However, in 2012 (Fig. 1b) no statistically significant differences were obtained by using PM, and MY was reduced on average by -4.86 %. Nevertheless, the aggregated results obtained from the three-year experiment (Fig. 1d) showed that the application of PM contributed to statistically significant increase in MY by 12.69 %, observed from all FMs. The highest and statistically significant increase in MY was recorded for FM1 and FM2 (35.91 % and 20.72 % respectively). In case when different FMs were applied, the effect of PM on MY was reduced, but it was still present and for certain treatments statistically significant. By using PM, the lowest difference in MY of 4.11 % was achieved for FM7 when combined application of CPM and MF were used.

A statistically significant effect of fertilization on MY increase was determined (Fig. 1d). The aggregated results show that fertilizing with MCM (FM2) and CPM (FM5) resulted in statistically significant increase in MY on average by 21.2% and 60.6% respectively, as compared to the treatment without fertilization (FM1) (Fig. 1d). Fertilization with CPM had a greater impact on the yield compared to MCM because CPM had higher content of N nutrients, P2O5, and K2O, and higher content of dry matter (Table 3). Fertilization with mineral fertilizers (FM8 and FM9) and increased fertilization rates (from 300 to 500 kg had also positive effect on yields and increased MY by 42.5-51.5 %. The highest value of MY was achieved by combined application of manure and mineral fertilizer (CPM and MF) in FM7 when an increase of 73.26 % was achieved as compared to FM1.

Statistically significant differences in MY were regularly determined when different FMs were applied, as compared to the treatment without fertilization (Fig. 1), but the same cannot be said for PM application. By analysing the effect of the FM X PM interaction on the variability of MY for each examined year separately (Fig. 1a,b,c) it was concluded that
Fig. 1. Spring cabbage marketable yield (MY) depending on fertilization methods, PM application and the year of the experiment.

statistically significant results were observed only in 2013 (SM 2.1; Table S26a, b, c). The effect of the FM X PM interaction on the vari- ability of MY on average over the years (Fig. 1d) was not statistically significant (SM 2.1; Table S26d); therefore, no additional testing was performed on this issue. The results of the multiple linear regression analysis showed that the model with the selected factors was statistically significant (SM 2.1; Table S27a) and that each of the selected factors (except MCM) statistically significantly influence the formation of MY (SM 2.1; Table S27b). Around 85 % of the variability of MY is explained by the influence of the selected independent variables (factors). Partial correlation coefficients show that the highest correlation values with MY were found for CPM (0.88) and MF (0.84), followed by PM (0.66), while MCM weakly correlated with MY (SM Table S27c). The results of the analysis showed that the obtained dependencies were nearly identical for TY and MY and the same conclusions can be reached regardless which yield is considered (SM 2.1; Table S25).

Dry matter content (DM) (SM 2.1; Table S25) is an important component of the yield quality of cabbage head. The use of PM and different FMs had a statistically significant effect on DM in each of the observed years. However, observing the three-year average results the changes in DM with regard to PM application and FMs were not statis- tically significant. The application of PM led to higher MY but slightly reduced DM from 7.62 %wb to 7.42 % which means that application of PM reduced DM by 2.63 %. Depending on the FMs, the highest aggregated value of DM was obtained for FM9 (7.91 %wb) and it was increased by 9.51 % compared to the control variant FM1 (7.25 %wb).

The effect of soil moisture on the MY between treatments, during the three observed years was minimal since reliable irrigation drip system was used and it enabled rational use and proper distribution of water on the plot. The use of PM resulted in reduction in total water use through the irrigation system by 13.64 % (SM 1.5; Table S7), and the total amount of water (irrigation + precipitation) during DAT was reduced from 420 to 393 mm, or by 6.35 %.
Mulching substantially reduced labor since savings in time necessary

for inter row cultivation, hoeing and irrigation exceeds the additional labor associated with PM application and collection. For the treatment w/o and w/ PM an average of 524 and 374 h ha-Superscript(1) labor was hired, respectively (SM 1.4 and 1.5; Table S5 and S7).
3.2. Environmental impact of cabbage production depending on PM and FMs application

The magnitude and the main contributors to the environmental damage caused by cabbage production are depicted in Fig. 2. Although the ReCiPe 2016 method calculates the impacts within 22 different endpoint categories (SM 2.2; Table S30 and S31), only few of them proved to be relevant in terms of their contribution to the overall LCIA results of cabbage production (Fig. 2). The combined effect of global warming and water consumption predominates in the total damage caused to human health and ecosystem quality. Non-carcinogenic toxicity and particulate matter formation are also important contribu- tors to human health impacts; whereas land use and water consumption have a significant share in the results for ecosystem quality indicator. The contribution of all other impact categories was minimal in com- parison to the depicted impact categories, which accounted for more than 90 % of the total impact in their respective area of protection. Damage caused to resource availability was almost fully associated with fossil energy depletion (Fig. 2).

The environmental benefits of PM application in respect to human health impacts were not unambiguous. Notable reductions in human health impacts were found only in FM1 and FM2. In FM4 the benefits from PM were negligible, whereas in other FMs an increase in human health damage was noticed when PM was applied (Fig. 2a). The benefits from mulching were more evident in the ecosystem quality damage category (Fig. 2b) where reductions in damages were observed regard- less of the FM being applied. Lower land use (interlinked with higher MY achieved with PM) and lower water requirements of options involving PM explain this observation. In terms of its impact on the depletion of
Damage to ecosystems quality (species X year)
Fig. 2. Environmental impact of PM on a) human heath, b) ecosystem quality and c) resource availability depending on the FM (per 1000 kg MY).

mineral resources (Fig. 2c), the application of PM resulted in higher impacts in all FMs, except for FM1. The relatively high energy demand of PM production (ca. 71 MJ kg -1 based on ecoinvent 3.4 data) completely offset the advantage of higher yields.

The three-year average results on the aggregated endpoint levels (Fig. 2) indicate that fertilization has contributed to the reduction of the environmental impact of cabbage regardless the FMs, since increased yields completely offset the increased damage caused by the production

and application of fertilizers. In cases when PM was not applied, the application of MCM reduced the environmental impact in the three aggregated damage categories for about 18-22 per unit of MY. With the use of CPM and MF, the damage to human health, ecosystems and resources were further reduced by 29-44 % as compared to FM1. Favorable environmental impact was also achieved by combined application of the mentioned fertilizers. In cases when PM was used, fertilization has also contributed to reduction in environmental impacts
per unit of MY, but the positive effect of fertilization was reduced by a third or even more than half as compared to the situation when PM was not applied.

A more detailed analyses of the three-year average results across the nine FMs (Fig. 3) revealed that PM application reduced water con- sumption non-carcinogenic toxicity (-25 %), terrestrial acidi- fication (-4%) and the required land area %), but increased fossil resource scarcity (+8 %), global warming (+6 9 and fine particulate matter emissions (+14%) per 1000 kg of MY (SM 2.2; Table S34). In two out of the seven relevant midpoint categories results were dominated with a single process or flow type. Namely, water used for irrigation of cabbage was responsible for 98 of WD, whereas around 95 of im- pacts associated with LU category were related to land area used for cabbage production. In other categories, the impacts were dominated with flows and processes associated with irrigation and fertilization (production of fertilizers and field emissions) followed by the produc- tion of seedlings and diesel used in agricultural machinery. Processes and flows associated with the production and disposal of PM made 8-22 % of total impacts in five out of the seven relevant categories (Fig. 3).

Minimal, maximal and average relative differences between the re- sults of midpoint category indicators across the nine FMs depending on whether PM was applied are shown in Fig. 4 (SM 2.2; Table S35). The results are expressed per unit of area which explains the increase in impact category results (indicated with positive values), due to appli- cation of PM in most of the midpoint impact categories. Results show that in 12 out of 18 midpoint categories considered by the ReCiPe 2016 method, the average increase in impacts caused by PM application is lower than the average increase in MYs attributable to PM application (SM 2.1; Fig. 1; Table S25) which means that yield increase due to PM is likely to counterbalance the negative effects of mulching in two-thirds of the investigated impact categories.

Fig. 5 shows the processes affected by mulching and their relative contribution to the overall impacts associated with PM in various midpoint impact categories, referring to average contributions across the nine FMs, while detailed LCIA data for each of the FM is available in the supplementary material (SM 2.2; Table S346). The Fig. 5 clearly depicts that application of PM had both positive and negative effects. The negative effects, indicated by positive values in Fig. 5, were mostly related to the production and end-of-life treatment of plastic mulch, whereas the positive effects arose from the lower consumption of

irrigation water, lower diesel fuel consumption in agricultural machin- ery and irrigation pumps, lower nitrogen emissions from soil and lower machinery use per unit of MY achieved. Around 51 %-98 of the total impacts induced by the application of PM was achieved by the pro- duction and end-of-life treatment of PM, except for water consumption, marine eutrophication, ozone formation and mineral depletion impact categories in which the PM related impacts made between 2% and one- third of the overall impacts. In the last two impact categories (OHH and MD), flows associated with the production of agricultural machines and diesel fuel usage caused a significant share in the overall impacts.
31: 3.1. Cabbage yield in function of plastic mulch and fertilization methods

The output from cabbage production is its total biological yield (TY), but marketable yield (MY), which represents the weight of the cabbage head, is more relevant, both from economic and LCA perspectives (SM 2.1; Table S25). Differences in MY depending on PM and FMs and their statistical significant differences at the 5 % significance level are shown in Fig. 1.

Depending on the year of investigation, in 2010 and 2012 (Fig. 1a,b), considerably higher average yields of cabbage head (MY) (48.45 and 55.40 t ha were achieved as compared to 2013 when the average yield was only 27.48 t ha- (Fig. 1c).

In 2010 and 2013 (Fig. 1a,c) statistically significant differences in MYs were observed between the treatments w/o and w/ PM, and the application of PM resulted on average yield increase (aggregated results observed from all FMs) of 21.08% and 26.14%, respectively. However, in 2012 (Fig. 1b) no statistically significant differences were obtained by using PM, and MY was reduced on average by -4.86 %. Nevertheless, the aggregated results obtained from the three-year experiment (Fig. 1d) showed that the application of PM contributed to statistically significant increase in MY by 12.69 %, observed from all FMs. The highest and statistically significant increase in MY was recorded for FM1 and FM2 (35.91 % and 20.72 % respectively). In case when different FMs were applied, the effect of PM on MY was reduced, but it was still present and for certain treatments statistically significant. By using PM, the lowest difference in MY of 4.11 % was achieved for FM7 when combined application of CPM and MF were used.

A statistically significant effect of fertilization on MY increase was determined (Fig. 1d). The aggregated results show that fertilizing with MCM (FM2) and CPM (FM5) resulted in statistically significant increase in MY on average by 21.2% and 60.6% respectively, as compared to the treatment without fertilization (FM1) (Fig. 1d). Fertilization with CPM had a greater impact on the yield compared to MCM because CPM had higher content of N nutrients, P2O5, and K2O, and higher content of dry matter (Table 3). Fertilization with mineral fertilizers (FM8 and FM9) and increased fertilization rates (from 300 to 500 kg had also positive effect on yields and increased MY by 42.5-51.5 %. The highest value of MY was achieved by combined application of manure and mineral fertilizer (CPM and MF) in FM7 when an increase of 73.26 % was achieved as compared to FM1.

Statistically significant differences in MY were regularly determined when different FMs were applied, as compared to the treatment without fertilization (Fig. 1), but the same cannot be said for PM application. By analysing the effect of the FM X PM interaction on the variability of MY for each examined year separately (Fig. 1a,b,c) it was concluded that
Fig. 1. Spring cabbage marketable yield (MY) depending on fertilization methods, PM application and the year of the experiment.

statistically significant results were observed only in 2013 (SM 2.1; Table S26a, b, c). The effect of the FM X PM interaction on the vari- ability of MY on average over the years (Fig. 1d) was not statistically significant (SM 2.1; Table S26d); therefore, no additional testing was performed on this issue. The results of the multiple linear regression analysis showed that the model with the selected factors was statistically significant (SM 2.1; Table S27a) and that each of the selected factors (except MCM) statistically significantly influence the formation of MY (SM 2.1; Table S27b). Around 85 % of the variability of MY is explained by the influence of the selected independent variables (factors). Partial correlation coefficients show that the highest correlation values with MY were found for CPM (0.88) and MF (0.84), followed by PM (0.66), while MCM weakly correlated with MY (SM Table S27c). The results of the analysis showed that the obtained dependencies were nearly identical for TY and MY and the same conclusions can be reached regardless which yield is considered (SM 2.1; Table S25).

Dry matter content (DM) (SM 2.1; Table S25) is an important component of the yield quality of cabbage head. The use of PM and different FMs had a statistically significant effect on DM in each of the observed years. However, observing the three-year average results the changes in DM with regard to PM application and FMs were not statis- tically significant. The application of PM led to higher MY but slightly reduced DM from 7.62 %wb to 7.42 % which means that application of PM reduced DM by 2.63 %. Depending on the FMs, the highest aggregated value of DM was obtained for FM9 (7.91 %wb) and it was increased by 9.51 % compared to the control variant FM1 (7.25 %wb).

The effect of soil moisture on the MY between treatments, during the three observed years was minimal since reliable irrigation drip system was used and it enabled rational use and proper distribution of water on the plot. The use of PM resulted in reduction in total water use through the irrigation system by 13.64 % (SM 1.5; Table S7), and the total amount of water (irrigation + precipitation) during DAT was reduced from 420 to 393 mm, or by 6.35 %.
Mulching substantially reduced labor since savings in time necessary

for inter row cultivation, hoeing and irrigation exceeds the additional labor associated with PM application and collection. For the treatment w/o and w/ PM an average of 524 and 374 h ha-Superscript(1) labor was hired, respectively (SM 1.4 and 1.5; Table S5 and S7).
3.2. Environmental impact of cabbage production depending on PM and FMs application

The magnitude and the main contributors to the environmental damage caused by cabbage production are depicted in Fig. 2. Although the ReCiPe 2016 method calculates the impacts within 22 different endpoint categories (SM 2.2; Table S30 and S31), only few of them proved to be relevant in terms of their contribution to the overall LCIA results of cabbage production (Fig. 2). The combined effect of global warming and water consumption predominates in the total damage caused to human health and ecosystem quality. Non-carcinogenic toxicity and particulate matter formation are also important contribu- tors to human health impacts; whereas land use and water consumption have a significant share in the results for ecosystem quality indicator. The contribution of all other impact categories was minimal in com- parison to the depicted impact categories, which accounted for more than 90 % of the total impact in their respective area of protection. Damage caused to resource availability was almost fully associated with fossil energy depletion (Fig. 2).

The environmental benefits of PM application in respect to human health impacts were not unambiguous. Notable reductions in human health impacts were found only in FM1 and FM2. In FM4 the benefits from PM were negligible, whereas in other FMs an increase in human health damage was noticed when PM was applied (Fig. 2a). The benefits from mulching were more evident in the ecosystem quality damage category (Fig. 2b) where reductions in damages were observed regard- less of the FM being applied. Lower land use (interlinked with higher MY achieved with PM) and lower water requirements of options involving PM explain this observation. In terms of its impact on the depletion of
Damage to ecosystems quality (species X year)
Fig. 2. Environmental impact of PM on a) human heath, b) ecosystem quality and c) resource availability depending on the FM (per 1000 kg MY).

mineral resources (Fig. 2c), the application of PM resulted in higher impacts in all FMs, except for FM1. The relatively high energy demand of PM production (ca. 71 MJ kg -1 based on ecoinvent 3.4 data) completely offset the advantage of higher yields.

The three-year average results on the aggregated endpoint levels (Fig. 2) indicate that fertilization has contributed to the reduction of the environmental impact of cabbage regardless the FMs, since increased yields completely offset the increased damage caused by the production

and application of fertilizers. In cases when PM was not applied, the application of MCM reduced the environmental impact in the three aggregated damage categories for about 18-22 per unit of MY. With the use of CPM and MF, the damage to human health, ecosystems and resources were further reduced by 29-44 % as compared to FM1. Favorable environmental impact was also achieved by combined application of the mentioned fertilizers. In cases when PM was used, fertilization has also contributed to reduction in environmental impacts
per unit of MY, but the positive effect of fertilization was reduced by a third or even more than half as compared to the situation when PM was not applied.

A more detailed analyses of the three-year average results across the nine FMs (Fig. 3) revealed that PM application reduced water con- sumption non-carcinogenic toxicity (-25 %), terrestrial acidi- fication (-4%) and the required land area %), but increased fossil resource scarcity (+8 %), global warming (+6 9 and fine particulate matter emissions (+14%) per 1000 kg of MY (SM 2.2; Table S34). In two out of the seven relevant midpoint categories results were dominated with a single process or flow type. Namely, water used for irrigation of cabbage was responsible for 98 of WD, whereas around 95 of im- pacts associated with LU category were related to land area used for cabbage production. In other categories, the impacts were dominated with flows and processes associated with irrigation and fertilization (production of fertilizers and field emissions) followed by the produc- tion of seedlings and diesel used in agricultural machinery. Processes and flows associated with the production and disposal of PM made 8-22 % of total impacts in five out of the seven relevant categories (Fig. 3).

Minimal, maximal and average relative differences between the re- sults of midpoint category indicators across the nine FMs depending on whether PM was applied are shown in Fig. 4 (SM 2.2; Table S35). The results are expressed per unit of area which explains the increase in impact category results (indicated with positive values), due to appli- cation of PM in most of the midpoint impact categories. Results show that in 12 out of 18 midpoint categories considered by the ReCiPe 2016 method, the average increase in impacts caused by PM application is lower than the average increase in MYs attributable to PM application (SM 2.1; Fig. 1; Table S25) which means that yield increase due to PM is likely to counterbalance the negative effects of mulching in two-thirds of the investigated impact categories.

Fig. 5 shows the processes affected by mulching and their relative contribution to the overall impacts associated with PM in various midpoint impact categories, referring to average contributions across the nine FMs, while detailed LCIA data for each of the FM is available in the supplementary material (SM 2.2; Table S346). The Fig. 5 clearly depicts that application of PM had both positive and negative effects. The negative effects, indicated by positive values in Fig. 5, were mostly related to the production and end-of-life treatment of plastic mulch, whereas the positive effects arose from the lower consumption of

irrigation water, lower diesel fuel consumption in agricultural machin- ery and irrigation pumps, lower nitrogen emissions from soil and lower machinery use per unit of MY achieved. Around 51 %-98 of the total impacts induced by the application of PM was achieved by the pro- duction and end-of-life treatment of PM, except for water consumption, marine eutrophication, ozone formation and mineral depletion impact categories in which the PM related impacts made between 2% and one- third of the overall impacts. In the last two impact categories (OHH and MD), flows associated with the production of agricultural machines and diesel fuel usage caused a significant share in the overall impacts.
34: 3.1. Cabbage yield in function of plastic mulch and fertilization methods

The output from cabbage production is its total biological yield (TY), but marketable yield (MY), which represents the weight of the cabbage head, is more relevant, both from economic and LCA perspectives (SM 2.1; Table S25). Differences in MY depending on PM and FMs and their statistical significant differences at the 5 % significance level are shown in Fig. 1.

Depending on the year of investigation, in 2010 and 2012 (Fig. 1a,b), considerably higher average yields of cabbage head (MY) (48.45 and 55.40 t ha were achieved as compared to 2013 when the average yield was only 27.48 t ha- (Fig. 1c).

In 2010 and 2013 (Fig. 1a,c) statistically significant differences in MYs were observed between the treatments w/o and w/ PM, and the application of PM resulted on average yield increase (aggregated results observed from all FMs) of 21.08% and 26.14%, respectively. However, in 2012 (Fig. 1b) no statistically significant differences were obtained by using PM, and MY was reduced on average by -4.86 %. Nevertheless, the aggregated results obtained from the three-year experiment (Fig. 1d) showed that the application of PM contributed to statistically significant increase in MY by 12.69 %, observed from all FMs. The highest and statistically significant increase in MY was recorded for FM1 and FM2 (35.91 % and 20.72 % respectively). In case when different FMs were applied, the effect of PM on MY was reduced, but it was still present and for certain treatments statistically significant. By using PM, the lowest difference in MY of 4.11 % was achieved for FM7 when combined application of CPM and MF were used.

A statistically significant effect of fertilization on MY increase was determined (Fig. 1d). The aggregated results show that fertilizing with MCM (FM2) and CPM (FM5) resulted in statistically significant increase in MY on average by 21.2% and 60.6% respectively, as compared to the treatment without fertilization (FM1) (Fig. 1d). Fertilization with CPM had a greater impact on the yield compared to MCM because CPM had higher content of N nutrients, P2O5, and K2O, and higher content of dry matter (Table 3). Fertilization with mineral fertilizers (FM8 and FM9) and increased fertilization rates (from 300 to 500 kg had also positive effect on yields and increased MY by 42.5-51.5 %. The highest value of MY was achieved by combined application of manure and mineral fertilizer (CPM and MF) in FM7 when an increase of 73.26 % was achieved as compared to FM1.

Statistically significant differences in MY were regularly determined when different FMs were applied, as compared to the treatment without fertilization (Fig. 1), but the same cannot be said for PM application. By analysing the effect of the FM X PM interaction on the variability of MY for each examined year separately (Fig. 1a,b,c) it was concluded that
Fig. 1. Spring cabbage marketable yield (MY) depending on fertilization methods, PM application and the year of the experiment.

statistically significant results were observed only in 2013 (SM 2.1; Table S26a, b, c). The effect of the FM X PM interaction on the vari- ability of MY on average over the years (Fig. 1d) was not statistically significant (SM 2.1; Table S26d); therefore, no additional testing was performed on this issue. The results of the multiple linear regression analysis showed that the model with the selected factors was statistically significant (SM 2.1; Table S27a) and that each of the selected factors (except MCM) statistically significantly influence the formation of MY (SM 2.1; Table S27b). Around 85 % of the variability of MY is explained by the influence of the selected independent variables (factors). Partial correlation coefficients show that the highest correlation values with MY were found for CPM (0.88) and MF (0.84), followed by PM (0.66), while MCM weakly correlated with MY (SM Table S27c). The results of the analysis showed that the obtained dependencies were nearly identical for TY and MY and the same conclusions can be reached regardless which yield is considered (SM 2.1; Table S25).

Dry matter content (DM) (SM 2.1; Table S25) is an important component of the yield quality of cabbage head. The use of PM and different FMs had a statistically significant effect on DM in each of the observed years. However, observing the three-year average results the changes in DM with regard to PM application and FMs were not statis- tically significant. The application of PM led to higher MY but slightly reduced DM from 7.62 %wb to 7.42 % which means that application of PM reduced DM by 2.63 %. Depending on the FMs, the highest aggregated value of DM was obtained for FM9 (7.91 %wb) and it was increased by 9.51 % compared to the control variant FM1 (7.25 %wb).

The effect of soil moisture on the MY between treatments, during the three observed years was minimal since reliable irrigation drip system was used and it enabled rational use and proper distribution of water on the plot. The use of PM resulted in reduction in total water use through the irrigation system by 13.64 % (SM 1.5; Table S7), and the total amount of water (irrigation + precipitation) during DAT was reduced from 420 to 393 mm, or by 6.35 %.
Mulching substantially reduced labor since savings in time necessary

for inter row cultivation, hoeing and irrigation exceeds the additional labor associated with PM application and collection. For the treatment w/o and w/ PM an average of 524 and 374 h ha-Superscript(1) labor was hired, respectively (SM 1.4 and 1.5; Table S5 and S7).
3.2. Environmental impact of cabbage production depending on PM and FMs application

The magnitude and the main contributors to the environmental damage caused by cabbage production are depicted in Fig. 2. Although the ReCiPe 2016 method calculates the impacts within 22 different endpoint categories (SM 2.2; Table S30 and S31), only few of them proved to be relevant in terms of their contribution to the overall LCIA results of cabbage production (Fig. 2). The combined effect of global warming and water consumption predominates in the total damage caused to human health and ecosystem quality. Non-carcinogenic toxicity and particulate matter formation are also important contribu- tors to human health impacts; whereas land use and water consumption have a significant share in the results for ecosystem quality indicator. The contribution of all other impact categories was minimal in com- parison to the depicted impact categories, which accounted for more than 90 % of the total impact in their respective area of protection. Damage caused to resource availability was almost fully associated with fossil energy depletion (Fig. 2).

The environmental benefits of PM application in respect to human health impacts were not unambiguous. Notable reductions in human health impacts were found only in FM1 and FM2. In FM4 the benefits from PM were negligible, whereas in other FMs an increase in human health damage was noticed when PM was applied (Fig. 2a). The benefits from mulching were more evident in the ecosystem quality damage category (Fig. 2b) where reductions in damages were observed regard- less of the FM being applied. Lower land use (interlinked with higher MY achieved with PM) and lower water requirements of options involving PM explain this observation. In terms of its impact on the depletion of
Damage to ecosystems quality (species X year)
Fig. 2. Environmental impact of PM on a) human heath, b) ecosystem quality and c) resource availability depending on the FM (per 1000 kg MY).

mineral resources (Fig. 2c), the application of PM resulted in higher impacts in all FMs, except for FM1. The relatively high energy demand of PM production (ca. 71 MJ kg -1 based on ecoinvent 3.4 data) completely offset the advantage of higher yields.

The three-year average results on the aggregated endpoint levels (Fig. 2) indicate that fertilization has contributed to the reduction of the environmental impact of cabbage regardless the FMs, since increased yields completely offset the increased damage caused by the production

and application of fertilizers. In cases when PM was not applied, the application of MCM reduced the environmental impact in the three aggregated damage categories for about 18-22 per unit of MY. With the use of CPM and MF, the damage to human health, ecosystems and resources were further reduced by 29-44 % as compared to FM1. Favorable environmental impact was also achieved by combined application of the mentioned fertilizers. In cases when PM was used, fertilization has also contributed to reduction in environmental impacts
per unit of MY, but the positive effect of fertilization was reduced by a third or even more than half as compared to the situation when PM was not applied.

A more detailed analyses of the three-year average results across the nine FMs (Fig. 3) revealed that PM application reduced water con- sumption non-carcinogenic toxicity (-25 %), terrestrial acidi- fication (-4%) and the required land area %), but increased fossil resource scarcity (+8 %), global warming (+6 9 and fine particulate matter emissions (+14%) per 1000 kg of MY (SM 2.2; Table S34). In two out of the seven relevant midpoint categories results were dominated with a single process or flow type. Namely, water used for irrigation of cabbage was responsible for 98 of WD, whereas around 95 of im- pacts associated with LU category were related to land area used for cabbage production. In other categories, the impacts were dominated with flows and processes associated with irrigation and fertilization (production of fertilizers and field emissions) followed by the produc- tion of seedlings and diesel used in agricultural machinery. Processes and flows associated with the production and disposal of PM made 8-22 % of total impacts in five out of the seven relevant categories (Fig. 3).

Minimal, maximal and average relative differences between the re- sults of midpoint category indicators across the nine FMs depending on whether PM was applied are shown in Fig. 4 (SM 2.2; Table S35). The results are expressed per unit of area which explains the increase in impact category results (indicated with positive values), due to appli- cation of PM in most of the midpoint impact categories. Results show that in 12 out of 18 midpoint categories considered by the ReCiPe 2016 method, the average increase in impacts caused by PM application is lower than the average increase in MYs attributable to PM application (SM 2.1; Fig. 1; Table S25) which means that yield increase due to PM is likely to counterbalance the negative effects of mulching in two-thirds of the investigated impact categories.

Fig. 5 shows the processes affected by mulching and their relative contribution to the overall impacts associated with PM in various midpoint impact categories, referring to average contributions across the nine FMs, while detailed LCIA data for each of the FM is available in the supplementary material (SM 2.2; Table S346). The Fig. 5 clearly depicts that application of PM had both positive and negative effects. The negative effects, indicated by positive values in Fig. 5, were mostly related to the production and end-of-life treatment of plastic mulch, whereas the positive effects arose from the lower consumption of

irrigation water, lower diesel fuel consumption in agricultural machin- ery and irrigation pumps, lower nitrogen emissions from soil and lower machinery use per unit of MY achieved. Around 51 %-98 of the total impacts induced by the application of PM was achieved by the pro- duction and end-of-life treatment of PM, except for water consumption, marine eutrophication, ozone formation and mineral depletion impact categories in which the PM related impacts made between 2% and one- third of the overall impacts. In the last two impact categories (OHH and MD), flows associated with the production of agricultural machines and diesel fuel usage caused a significant share in the overall impacts.
37: 3.1. Cabbage yield in function of plastic mulch and fertilization methods

The output from cabbage production is its total biological yield (TY), but marketable yield (MY), which represents the weight of the cabbage head, is more relevant, both from economic and LCA perspectives (SM 2.1; Table S25). Differences in MY depending on PM and FMs and their statistical significant differences at the 5 % significance level are shown in Fig. 1.

Depending on the year of investigation, in 2010 and 2012 (Fig. 1a,b), considerably higher average yields of cabbage head (MY) (48.45 and 55.40 t ha were achieved as compared to 2013 when the average yield was only 27.48 t ha- (Fig. 1c).

In 2010 and 2013 (Fig. 1a,c) statistically significant differences in MYs were observed between the treatments w/o and w/ PM, and the application of PM resulted on average yield increase (aggregated results observed from all FMs) of 21.08% and 26.14%, respectively. However, in 2012 (Fig. 1b) no statistically significant differences were obtained by using PM, and MY was reduced on average by -4.86 %. Nevertheless, the aggregated results obtained from the three-year experiment (Fig. 1d) showed that the application of PM contributed to statistically significant increase in MY by 12.69 %, observed from all FMs. The highest and statistically significant increase in MY was recorded for FM1 and FM2 (35.91 % and 20.72 % respectively). In case when different FMs were applied, the effect of PM on MY was reduced, but it was still present and for certain treatments statistically significant. By using PM, the lowest difference in MY of 4.11 % was achieved for FM7 when combined application of CPM and MF were used.

A statistically significant effect of fertilization on MY increase was determined (Fig. 1d). The aggregated results show that fertilizing with MCM (FM2) and CPM (FM5) resulted in statistically significant increase in MY on average by 21.2% and 60.6% respectively, as compared to the treatment without fertilization (FM1) (Fig. 1d). Fertilization with CPM had a greater impact on the yield compared to MCM because CPM had higher content of N nutrients, P2O5, and K2O, and higher content of dry matter (Table 3). Fertilization with mineral fertilizers (FM8 and FM9) and increased fertilization rates (from 300 to 500 kg had also positive effect on yields and increased MY by 42.5-51.5 %. The highest value of MY was achieved by combined application of manure and mineral fertilizer (CPM and MF) in FM7 when an increase of 73.26 % was achieved as compared to FM1.

Statistically significant differences in MY were regularly determined when different FMs were applied, as compared to the treatment without fertilization (Fig. 1), but the same cannot be said for PM application. By analysing the effect of the FM X PM interaction on the variability of MY for each examined year separately (Fig. 1a,b,c) it was concluded that
Fig. 1. Spring cabbage marketable yield (MY) depending on fertilization methods, PM application and the year of the experiment.

statistically significant results were observed only in 2013 (SM 2.1; Table S26a, b, c). The effect of the FM X PM interaction on the vari- ability of MY on average over the years (Fig. 1d) was not statistically significant (SM 2.1; Table S26d); therefore, no additional testing was performed on this issue. The results of the multiple linear regression analysis showed that the model with the selected factors was statistically significant (SM 2.1; Table S27a) and that each of the selected factors (except MCM) statistically significantly influence the formation of MY (SM 2.1; Table S27b). Around 85 % of the variability of MY is explained by the influence of the selected independent variables (factors). Partial correlation coefficients show that the highest correlation values with MY were found for CPM (0.88) and MF (0.84), followed by PM (0.66), while MCM weakly correlated with MY (SM Table S27c). The results of the analysis showed that the obtained dependencies were nearly identical for TY and MY and the same conclusions can be reached regardless which yield is considered (SM 2.1; Table S25).

Dry matter content (DM) (SM 2.1; Table S25) is an important component of the yield quality of cabbage head. The use of PM and different FMs had a statistically significant effect on DM in each of the observed years. However, observing the three-year average results the changes in DM with regard to PM application and FMs were not statis- tically significant. The application of PM led to higher MY but slightly reduced DM from 7.62 %wb to 7.42 % which means that application of PM reduced DM by 2.63 %. Depending on the FMs, the highest aggregated value of DM was obtained for FM9 (7.91 %wb) and it was increased by 9.51 % compared to the control variant FM1 (7.25 %wb).

The effect of soil moisture on the MY between treatments, during the three observed years was minimal since reliable irrigation drip system was used and it enabled rational use and proper distribution of water on the plot. The use of PM resulted in reduction in total water use through the irrigation system by 13.64 % (SM 1.5; Table S7), and the total amount of water (irrigation + precipitation) during DAT was reduced from 420 to 393 mm, or by 6.35 %.
Mulching substantially reduced labor since savings in time necessary

for inter row cultivation, hoeing and irrigation exceeds the additional labor associated with PM application and collection. For the treatment w/o and w/ PM an average of 524 and 374 h ha-Superscript(1) labor was hired, respectively (SM 1.4 and 1.5; Table S5 and S7).
3.2. Environmental impact of cabbage production depending on PM and FMs application

The magnitude and the main contributors to the environmental damage caused by cabbage production are depicted in Fig. 2. Although the ReCiPe 2016 method calculates the impacts within 22 different endpoint categories (SM 2.2; Table S30 and S31), only few of them proved to be relevant in terms of their contribution to the overall LCIA results of cabbage production (Fig. 2). The combined effect of global warming and water consumption predominates in the total damage caused to human health and ecosystem quality. Non-carcinogenic toxicity and particulate matter formation are also important contribu- tors to human health impacts; whereas land use and water consumption have a significant share in the results for ecosystem quality indicator. The contribution of all other impact categories was minimal in com- parison to the depicted impact categories, which accounted for more than 90 % of the total impact in their respective area of protection. Damage caused to resource availability was almost fully associated with fossil energy depletion (Fig. 2).

The environmental benefits of PM application in respect to human health impacts were not unambiguous. Notable reductions in human health impacts were found only in FM1 and FM2. In FM4 the benefits from PM were negligible, whereas in other FMs an increase in human health damage was noticed when PM was applied (Fig. 2a). The benefits from mulching were more evident in the ecosystem quality damage category (Fig. 2b) where reductions in damages were observed regard- less of the FM being applied. Lower land use (interlinked with higher MY achieved with PM) and lower water requirements of options involving PM explain this observation. In terms of its impact on the depletion of
Damage to ecosystems quality (species X year)
Fig. 2. Environmental impact of PM on a) human heath, b) ecosystem quality and c) resource availability depending on the FM (per 1000 kg MY).

mineral resources (Fig. 2c), the application of PM resulted in higher impacts in all FMs, except for FM1. The relatively high energy demand of PM production (ca. 71 MJ kg -1 based on ecoinvent 3.4 data) completely offset the advantage of higher yields.

The three-year average results on the aggregated endpoint levels (Fig. 2) indicate that fertilization has contributed to the reduction of the environmental impact of cabbage regardless the FMs, since increased yields completely offset the increased damage caused by the production

and application of fertilizers. In cases when PM was not applied, the application of MCM reduced the environmental impact in the three aggregated damage categories for about 18-22 per unit of MY. With the use of CPM and MF, the damage to human health, ecosystems and resources were further reduced by 29-44 % as compared to FM1. Favorable environmental impact was also achieved by combined application of the mentioned fertilizers. In cases when PM was used, fertilization has also contributed to reduction in environmental impacts
per unit of MY, but the positive effect of fertilization was reduced by a third or even more than half as compared to the situation when PM was not applied.

A more detailed analyses of the three-year average results across the nine FMs (Fig. 3) revealed that PM application reduced water con- sumption non-carcinogenic toxicity (-25 %), terrestrial acidi- fication (-4%) and the required land area %), but increased fossil resource scarcity (+8 %), global warming (+6 9 and fine particulate matter emissions (+14%) per 1000 kg of MY (SM 2.2; Table S34). In two out of the seven relevant midpoint categories results were dominated with a single process or flow type. Namely, water used for irrigation of cabbage was responsible for 98 of WD, whereas around 95 of im- pacts associated with LU category were related to land area used for cabbage production. In other categories, the impacts were dominated with flows and processes associated with irrigation and fertilization (production of fertilizers and field emissions) followed by the produc- tion of seedlings and diesel used in agricultural machinery. Processes and flows associated with the production and disposal of PM made 8-22 % of total impacts in five out of the seven relevant categories (Fig. 3).

Minimal, maximal and average relative differences between the re- sults of midpoint category indicators across the nine FMs depending on whether PM was applied are shown in Fig. 4 (SM 2.2; Table S35). The results are expressed per unit of area which explains the increase in impact category results (indicated with positive values), due to appli- cation of PM in most of the midpoint impact categories. Results show that in 12 out of 18 midpoint categories considered by the ReCiPe 2016 method, the average increase in impacts caused by PM application is lower than the average increase in MYs attributable to PM application (SM 2.1; Fig. 1; Table S25) which means that yield increase due to PM is likely to counterbalance the negative effects of mulching in two-thirds of the investigated impact categories.

Fig. 5 shows the processes affected by mulching and their relative contribution to the overall impacts associated with PM in various midpoint impact categories, referring to average contributions across the nine FMs, while detailed LCIA data for each of the FM is available in the supplementary material (SM 2.2; Table S346). The Fig. 5 clearly depicts that application of PM had both positive and negative effects. The negative effects, indicated by positive values in Fig. 5, were mostly related to the production and end-of-life treatment of plastic mulch, whereas the positive effects arose from the lower consumption of

irrigation water, lower diesel fuel consumption in agricultural machin- ery and irrigation pumps, lower nitrogen emissions from soil and lower machinery use per unit of MY achieved. Around 51 %-98 of the total impacts induced by the application of PM was achieved by the pro- duction and end-of-life treatment of PM, except for water consumption, marine eutrophication, ozone formation and mineral depletion impact categories in which the PM related impacts made between 2% and one- third of the overall impacts. In the last two impact categories (OHH and MD), flows associated with the production of agricultural machines and diesel fuel usage caused a significant share in the overall impacts.
40: 3.1. Cabbage yield in function of plastic mulch and fertilization methods

The output from cabbage production is its total biological yield (TY), but marketable yield (MY), which represents the weight of the cabbage head, is more relevant, both from economic and LCA perspectives (SM 2.1; Table S25). Differences in MY depending on PM and FMs and their statistical significant differences at the 5 % significance level are shown in Fig. 1.

Depending on the year of investigation, in 2010 and 2012 (Fig. 1a,b), considerably higher average yields of cabbage head (MY) (48.45 and 55.40 t ha were achieved as compared to 2013 when the average yield was only 27.48 t ha- (Fig. 1c).

In 2010 and 2013 (Fig. 1a,c) statistically significant differences in MYs were observed between the treatments w/o and w/ PM, and the application of PM resulted on average yield increase (aggregated results observed from all FMs) of 21.08% and 26.14%, respectively. However, in 2012 (Fig. 1b) no statistically significant differences were obtained by using PM, and MY was reduced on average by -4.86 %. Nevertheless, the aggregated results obtained from the three-year experiment (Fig. 1d) showed that the application of PM contributed to statistically significant increase in MY by 12.69 %, observed from all FMs. The highest and statistically significant increase in MY was recorded for FM1 and FM2 (35.91 % and 20.72 % respectively). In case when different FMs were applied, the effect of PM on MY was reduced, but it was still present and for certain treatments statistically significant. By using PM, the lowest difference in MY of 4.11 % was achieved for FM7 when combined application of CPM and MF were used.

A statistically significant effect of fertilization on MY increase was determined (Fig. 1d). The aggregated results show that fertilizing with MCM (FM2) and CPM (FM5) resulted in statistically significant increase in MY on average by 21.2% and 60.6% respectively, as compared to the treatment without fertilization (FM1) (Fig. 1d). Fertilization with CPM had a greater impact on the yield compared to MCM because CPM had higher content of N nutrients, P2O5, and K2O, and higher content of dry matter (Table 3). Fertilization with mineral fertilizers (FM8 and FM9) and increased fertilization rates (from 300 to 500 kg had also positive effect on yields and increased MY by 42.5-51.5 %. The highest value of MY was achieved by combined application of manure and mineral fertilizer (CPM and MF) in FM7 when an increase of 73.26 % was achieved as compared to FM1.

Statistically significant differences in MY were regularly determined when different FMs were applied, as compared to the treatment without fertilization (Fig. 1), but the same cannot be said for PM application. By analysing the effect of the FM X PM interaction on the variability of MY for each examined year separately (Fig. 1a,b,c) it was concluded that
Fig. 1. Spring cabbage marketable yield (MY) depending on fertilization methods, PM application and the year of the experiment.

statistically significant results were observed only in 2013 (SM 2.1; Table S26a, b, c). The results of the multiple linear regression analysis showed that the model with the selected factors was statistically significant (SM 2.1; Table S27a) and that each of the selected factors (except MCM) statistically significantly influence the formation of MY (SM 2.1; Table S27b). Around 85 % of the variability of MY is explained by the influence of the selected independent variables (factors). Partial correlation coefficients show that the highest correlation values with MY were found for CPM (0.88) and MF (0.84), followed by PM (0.66), while MCM weakly correlated with MY (SM Table S27c). The results of the analysis showed that the obtained dependencies were nearly identical for TY and MY and the same conclusions can be reached regardless which yield is considered (SM 2.1; Table S25).

Dry matter content (DM) (SM 2.1; Table S25) is an important component of the yield quality of cabbage head. The use of PM and different FMs had a statistically significant effect on DM in each of the observed years. However, observing the three-year average results the changes in DM with regard to PM application and FMs were not statis- tically significant. The application of PM led to higher MY but slightly reduced DM from 7.62 %wb to 7.42 % which means that application of PM reduced DM by 2.63 %. Depending on the FMs, the highest aggregated value of DM was obtained for FM9 (7.91 %wb) and it was increased by 9.51 % compared to the control variant FM1 (7.25 %wb).

The effect of soil moisture on the MY between treatments, during the three observed years was minimal since reliable irrigation drip system was used and it enabled rational use and proper distribution of water on the plot. The use of PM resulted in reduction in total water use through the irrigation system by 13.64 % (SM 1.5; Table S7), and the total amount of water (irrigation + precipitation) during DAT was reduced from 420 to 393 mm, or by 6.35 %.
Mulching substantially reduced labor since savings in time necessary

for inter row cultivation, hoeing and irrigation exceeds the additional labor associated with PM application and collection. For the treatment w/o and w/ PM an average of 524 and 374 h ha-Superscript(1) labor was hired, respectively (SM 1.4 and 1.5; Table S5 and S7).
3.2. Environmental impact of cabbage production depending on PM and FMs application

The magnitude and the main contributors to the environmental damage caused by cabbage production are depicted in Fig. 2. Although the ReCiPe 2016 method calculates the impacts within 22 different endpoint categories (SM 2.2; Table S30 and S31), only few of them proved to be relevant in terms of their contribution to the overall LCIA results of cabbage production (Fig. 2). The combined effect of global warming and water consumption predominates in the total damage caused to human health and ecosystem quality. Non-carcinogenic toxicity and particulate matter formation are also important contribu- tors to human health impacts; whereas land use and water consumption have a significant share in the results for ecosystem quality indicator. The contribution of all other impact categories was minimal in com- parison to the depicted impact categories, which accounted for more than 90 % of the total impact in their respective area of protection. Damage caused to resource availability was almost fully associated with fossil energy depletion (Fig. 2).

The environmental benefits of PM application in respect to human health impacts were not unambiguous. Notable reductions in human health impacts were found only in FM1 and FM2. In FM4 the benefits from PM were negligible, whereas in other FMs an increase in human health damage was noticed when PM was applied (Fig. 2a). The benefits from mulching were more evident in the ecosystem quality damage category (Fig. 2b) where reductions in damages were observed regard- less of the FM being applied. Lower land use (interlinked with higher MY achieved with PM) and lower water requirements of options involving PM explain this observation. In terms of its impact on the depletion of
Damage to ecosystems quality (species X year)
Fig. 2. Environmental impact of PM on a) human heath, b) ecosystem quality and c) resource availability depending on the FM (per 1000 kg MY).

mineral resources (Fig. 2c), the application of PM resulted in higher impacts in all FMs, except for FM1. The relatively high energy demand of PM production (ca. 71 MJ kg -1 based on ecoinvent 3.4 data) completely offset the advantage of higher yields.

The three-year average results on the aggregated endpoint levels (Fig. 2) indicate that fertilization has contributed to the reduction of the environmental impact of cabbage regardless the FMs, since increased yields completely offset the increased damage caused by the production

and application of fertilizers. In cases when PM was not applied, the application of MCM reduced the environmental impact in the three aggregated damage categories for about 18-22 per unit of MY. With the use of CPM and MF, the damage to human health, ecosystems and resources were further reduced by 29-44 % as compared to FM1. Favorable environmental impact was also achieved by combined application of the mentioned fertilizers. In cases when PM was used, fertilization has also contributed to reduction in environmental impacts
per unit of MY, but the positive effect of fertilization was reduced by a third or even more than half as compared to the situation when PM was not applied.

A more detailed analyses of the three-year average results across the nine FMs (Fig. 3) revealed that PM application reduced water con- sumption non-carcinogenic toxicity (-25 %), terrestrial acidi- fication (-4%) and the required land area %), but increased fossil resource scarcity (+8 %), global warming (+6 9 and fine particulate matter emissions (+14%) per 1000 kg of MY (SM 2.2; Table S34). In two out of the seven relevant midpoint categories results were dominated with a single process or flow type. Namely, water used for irrigation of cabbage was responsible for 98 of WD, whereas around 95 of im- pacts associated with LU category were related to land area used for cabbage production. In other categories, the impacts were dominated with flows and processes associated with irrigation and fertilization (production of fertilizers and field emissions) followed by the produc- tion of seedlings and diesel used in agricultural machinery. Processes and flows associated with the production and disposal of PM made 8-22 % of total impacts in five out of the seven relevant categories (Fig. 3).

Minimal, maximal and average relative differences between the re- sults of midpoint category indicators across the nine FMs depending on whether PM was applied are shown in Fig. 4 (SM 2.2; Table S35). The results are expressed per unit of area which explains the increase in impact category results (indicated with positive values), due to appli- cation of PM in most of the midpoint impact categories. Results show that in 12 out of 18 midpoint categories considered by the ReCiPe 2016 method, the average increase in impacts caused by PM application is lower than the average increase in MYs attributable to PM application (SM 2.1; Fig. 1; Table S25) which means that yield increase due to PM is likely to counterbalance the negative effects of mulching in two-thirds of the investigated impact categories.

Fig. 5 shows the processes affected by mulching and their relative contribution to the overall impacts associated with PM in various midpoint impact categories, referring to average contributions across the nine FMs, while detailed LCIA data for each of the FM is available in the supplementary material (SM 2.2; Table S346). The Fig. 5 clearly depicts that application of PM had both positive and negative effects. The negative effects, indicated by positive values in Fig. 5, were mostly related to the production and end-of-life treatment of plastic mulch, whereas the positive effects arose from the lower consumption of

irrigation water, lower diesel fuel consumption in agricultural machin- ery and irrigation pumps, lower nitrogen emissions from soil and lower machinery use per unit of MY achieved. Around 51 %-98 of the total impacts induced by the application of PM was achieved by the pro- duction and end-of-life treatment of PM, except for water consumption, marine eutrophication, ozone formation and mineral depletion impact categories in which the PM related impacts made between 2% and one- third of the overall impacts. In the last two impact categories (OHH and MD), flows associated with the production of agricultural machines and diesel fuel usage caused a significant share in the overall impacts.
43: 3.1. Cabbage yield in function of plastic mulch and fertilization methods

The output from cabbage production is its total biological yield (TY), but marketable yield (MY), which represents the weight of the cabbage head, is more relevant, both from economic and LCA perspectives (SM 2.1; Table S25). Differences in MY depending on PM and FMs and their statistical significant differences at the 5 % significance level are shown in Fig. 1.

Depending on the year of investigation, in 2010 and 2012 (Fig. 1a,b), considerably higher average yields of cabbage head (MY) (48.45 and 55.40 t ha were achieved as compared to 2013 when the average yield was only 27.48 t ha- (Fig. 1c).

In 2010 and 2013 (Fig. 1a,c) statistically significant differences in MYs were observed between the treatments w/o and w/ PM, and the application of PM resulted on average yield increase (aggregated results observed from all FMs) of 21.08% and 26.14%, respectively. However, in 2012 (Fig. 1b) no statistically significant differences were obtained by using PM, and MY was reduced on average by -4.86 %. Nevertheless, the aggregated results obtained from the three-year experiment (Fig. 1d) showed that the application of PM contributed to statistically significant increase in MY by 12.69 %, observed from all FMs. The highest and statistically significant increase in MY was recorded for FM1 and FM2 (35.91 % and 20.72 % respectively). In case when different FMs were applied, the effect of PM on MY was reduced, but it was still present and for certain treatments statistically significant. By using PM, the lowest difference in MY of 4.11 % was achieved for FM7 when combined application of CPM and MF were used.

A statistically significant effect of fertilization on MY increase was determined (Fig. 1d). The aggregated results show that fertilizing with MCM (FM2) and CPM (FM5) resulted in statistically significant increase in MY on average by 21.2% and 60.6% respectively, as compared to the treatment without fertilization (FM1) (Fig. 1d). Fertilization with CPM had a greater impact on the yield compared to MCM because CPM had higher content of N nutrients, P2O5, and K2O, and higher content of dry matter (Table 3). Fertilization with mineral fertilizers (FM8 and FM9) and increased fertilization rates (from 300 to 500 kg had also positive effect on yields and increased MY by 42.5-51.5 %. The highest value of MY was achieved by combined application of manure and mineral fertilizer (CPM and MF) in FM7 when an increase of 73.26 % was achieved as compared to FM1.

Statistically significant differences in MY were regularly determined when different FMs were applied, as compared to the treatment without fertilization (Fig. 1), but the same cannot be said for PM application. By analysing the effect of the FM X PM interaction on the variability of MY for each examined year separately (Fig. 1a,b,c) it was concluded that
Fig. 1. Spring cabbage marketable yield (MY) depending on fertilization methods, PM application and the year of the experiment.

statistically significant results were observed only in 2013 (SM 2.1; Table S26a, b, c). The effect of the FM X PM interaction on the vari- ability of MY on average over the years (Fig. 1d) was not statistically significant (SM 2.1; Table S26d); therefore, no additional testing was performed on this issue. The results of the multiple linear regression analysis showed that the model with the selected factors was statistically significant (SM 2.1; Table S27a) and that each of the selected factors (except MCM) statistically significantly influence the formation of MY (SM 2.1; Table S27b). Around 85 % of the variability of MY is explained by the influence of the selected independent variables (factors). Partial correlation coefficients show that the highest correlation values with MY were found for CPM (0.88) and MF (0.84), followed by PM (0.66), while MCM weakly correlated with MY (SM Table S27c). The results of the analysis showed that the obtained dependencies were nearly identical for TY and MY and the same conclusions can be reached regardless which yield is considered (SM 2.1; Table S25).

Dry matter content (DM) (SM 2.1; Table S25) is an important component of the yield quality of cabbage head. The use of PM and different FMs had a statistically significant effect on DM in each of the observed years. However, observing the three-year average results the changes in DM with regard to PM application and FMs were not statis- tically significant. The application of PM led to higher MY but slightly reduced DM from 7.62 %wb to 7.42 % which means that application of PM reduced DM by 2.63 %. Depending on the FMs, the highest aggregated value of DM was obtained for FM9 (7.91 %wb) and it was increased by 9.51 % compared to the control variant FM1 (7.25 %wb).

The effect of soil moisture on the MY between treatments, during the three observed years was minimal since reliable irrigation drip system was used and it enabled rational use and proper distribution of water on the plot. The use of PM resulted in reduction in total water use through the irrigation system by 13.64 % (SM 1.5; Table S7), and the total amount of water (irrigation + precipitation) during DAT was reduced from 420 to 393 mm, or by 6.35 %.
Mulching substantially reduced labor since savings in time necessary

for inter row cultivation, hoeing and irrigation exceeds the additional labor associated with PM application and collection. For the treatment w/o and w/ PM an average of 524 and 374 h ha-Superscript(1) labor was hired, respectively (SM 1.4 and 1.5; Table S5 and S7).
3.2. Environmental impact of cabbage production depending on PM and FMs application

The magnitude and the main contributors to the environmental damage caused by cabbage production are depicted in Fig. 2. Although the ReCiPe 2016 method calculates the impacts within 22 different endpoint categories (SM 2.2; Table S30 and S31), only few of them proved to be relevant in terms of their contribution to the overall LCIA results of cabbage production (Fig. 2). The combined effect of global warming and water consumption predominates in the total damage caused to human health and ecosystem quality. Non-carcinogenic toxicity and particulate matter formation are also important contribu- tors to human health impacts; whereas land use and water consumption have a significant share in the results for ecosystem quality indicator. The contribution of all other impact categories was minimal in com- parison to the depicted impact categories, which accounted for more than 90 % of the total impact in their respective area of protection. Damage caused to resource availability was almost fully associated with fossil energy depletion (Fig. 2).

The environmental benefits of PM application in respect to human health impacts were not unambiguous. Notable reductions in human health impacts were found only in FM1 and FM2. In FM4 the benefits from PM were negligible, whereas in other FMs an increase in human health damage was noticed when PM was applied (Fig. 2a). The benefits from mulching were more evident in the ecosystem quality damage category (Fig. 2b) where reductions in damages were observed regard- less of the FM being applied. Lower land use (interlinked with higher MY achieved with PM) and lower water requirements of options involving PM explain this observation. In terms of its impact on the depletion of
Damage to ecosystems quality (species X year)
Fig. 2. Environmental impact of PM on a) human heath, b) ecosystem quality and c) resource availability depending on the FM (per 1000 kg MY).

mineral resources (Fig. 2c), the application of PM resulted in higher impacts in all FMs, except for FM1. The relatively high energy demand of PM production (ca. 71 MJ kg -1 based on ecoinvent 3.4 data) completely offset the advantage of higher yields.

The three-year average results on the aggregated endpoint levels (Fig. 2) indicate that fertilization has contributed to the reduction of the environmental impact of cabbage regardless the FMs, since increased yields completely offset the increased damage caused by the production

and application of fertilizers. In cases when PM was not applied, the application of MCM reduced the environmental impact in the three aggregated damage categories for about 18-22 per unit of MY. With the use of CPM and MF, the damage to human health, ecosystems and resources were further reduced by 29-44 % as compared to FM1. Favorable environmental impact was also achieved by combined application of the mentioned fertilizers. In cases when PM was used, fertilization has also contributed to reduction in environmental impacts
per unit of MY, but the positive effect of fertilization was reduced by a third or even more than half as compared to the situation when PM was not applied.

A more detailed analyses of the three-year average results across the nine FMs (Fig. 3) revealed that PM application reduced water con- sumption non-carcinogenic toxicity (-25 %), terrestrial acidi- fication (-4%) and the required land area %), but increased fossil resource scarcity (+8 %), global warming (+6 9 and fine particulate matter emissions (+14%) per 1000 kg of MY (SM 2.2; Table S34). In two out of the seven relevant midpoint categories results were dominated with a single process or flow type. Namely, water used for irrigation of cabbage was responsible for 98 of WD, whereas around 95 of im- pacts associated with LU category were related to land area used for cabbage production. In other categories, the impacts were dominated with flows and processes associated with irrigation and fertilization (production of fertilizers and field emissions) followed by the produc- tion of seedlings and diesel used in agricultural machinery. Processes and flows associated with the production and disposal of PM made 8-22 % of total impacts in five out of the seven relevant categories (Fig. 3).

Minimal, maximal and average relative differences between the re- sults of midpoint category indicators across the nine FMs depending on whether PM was applied are shown in Fig. 4 (SM 2.2; Table S35). The results are expressed per unit of area which explains the increase in impact category results (indicated with positive values), due to appli- cation of PM in most of the midpoint impact categories. Results show that in 12 out of 18 midpoint categories considered by the ReCiPe 2016 method, the average increase in impacts caused by PM application is lower than the average increase in MYs attributable to PM application (SM 2.1; Fig. 1; Table S25) which means that yield increase due to PM is likely to counterbalance the negative effects of mulching in two-thirds of the investigated impact categories.

Fig. 5 shows the processes affected by mulching and their relative contribution to the overall impacts associated with PM in various midpoint impact categories, referring to average contributions across the nine FMs, while detailed LCIA data for each of the FM is available in the supplementary material (SM 2.2; Table S346). The Fig. 5 clearly depicts that application of PM had both positive and negative effects. The negative effects, indicated by positive values in Fig. 5, were mostly related to the production and end-of-life treatment of plastic mulch, whereas the positive effects arose from the lower consumption of

irrigation water, lower diesel fuel consumption in agricultural machin- ery and irrigation pumps, lower nitrogen emissions from soil and lower machinery use per unit of MY achieved. Around 51 %-98 of the total impacts induced by the application of PM was achieved by the pro- duction and end-of-life treatment of PM, except for water consumption, marine eutrophication, ozone formation and mineral depletion impact categories in which the PM related impacts made between 2% and one- third of the overall impacts. In the last two impact categories (OHH and MD), flows associated with the production of agricultural machines and diesel fuel usage caused a significant share in the overall impacts.
46: 3.1. Cabbage yield in function of plastic mulch and fertilization methods

The output from cabbage production is its total biological yield (TY), but marketable yield (MY), which represents the weight of the cabbage head, is more relevant, both from economic and LCA perspectives (SM 2.1; Table S25). Differences in MY depending on PM and FMs and their statistical significant differences at the 5 % significance level are shown in Fig. 1.

Depending on the year of investigation, in 2010 and 2012 (Fig. 1a,b), considerably higher average yields of cabbage head (MY) (48.45 and 55.40 t ha were achieved as compared to 2013 when the average yield was only 27.48 t ha- (Fig. 1c).

In 2010 and 2013 (Fig. 1a,c) statistically significant differences in MYs were observed between the treatments w/o and w/ PM, and the application of PM resulted on average yield increase (aggregated results observed from all FMs) of 21.08% and 26.14%, respectively. However, in 2012 (Fig. 1b) no statistically significant differences were obtained by using PM, and MY was reduced on average by -4.86 %. Nevertheless, the aggregated results obtained from the three-year experiment (Fig. 1d) showed that the application of PM contributed to statistically significant increase in MY by 12.69 %, observed from all FMs. The highest and statistically significant increase in MY was recorded for FM1 and FM2 (35.91 % and 20.72 % respectively). In case when different FMs were applied, the effect of PM on MY was reduced, but it was still present and for certain treatments statistically significant. By using PM, the lowest difference in MY of 4.11 % was achieved for FM7 when combined application of CPM and MF were used.

A statistically significant effect of fertilization on MY increase was determined (Fig. 1d). The aggregated results show that fertilizing with MCM (FM2) and CPM (FM5) resulted in statistically significant increase in MY on average by 21.2% and 60.6% respectively, as compared to the treatment without fertilization (FM1) (Fig. 1d). Fertilization with CPM had a greater impact on the yield compared to MCM because CPM had higher content of N nutrients, P2O5, and K2O, and higher content of dry matter (Table 3). Fertilization with mineral fertilizers (FM8 and FM9) and increased fertilization rates (from 300 to 500 kg had also positive effect on yields and increased MY by 42.5-51.5 %. The highest value of MY was achieved by combined application of manure and mineral fertilizer (CPM and MF) in FM7 when an increase of 73.26 % was achieved as compared to FM1.

Statistically significant differences in MY were regularly determined when different FMs were applied, as compared to the treatment without fertilization (Fig. 1), but the same cannot be said for PM application. By analysing the effect of the FM X PM interaction on the variability of MY for each examined year separately (Fig. 1a,b,c) it was concluded that

47: A series of sensitivity analyses were performed to test the influence of main assumptions and to identify key parameters that affect LCA results. The highest deviations in results were observed when different alloca- tion methods were used. Assuming that all emissions associated with manure application are allocated to cabbage (no-allocation scenario), the human health impact of cabbage would increase on average by 42 as compared to the default scenario which assumes N-based allocation. On the other hand, the choice between the nitrogen- and time-based allocation procedures would have only a minor impact on LCIA results (up to 5 %, Table 4).

Application of PM with thickness of 8 instead of 20 um, as commonly used in China (Changrong et al., 2014; Liu et al., 2015; Mo et al., 2016), would reduce the mass of PM applied. Assuming that there is no dif- ference between the environmental impacts of producing 1 kg of PM of different thicknesses, the application of PM with thickness of 8 um could reduce the environmental impact of cabbage production on human health and resource availability on average by 10 % and 12 respec- tively (Table 6).

The emission factors for total PAHs emissions from open-field com- bustion of PM varied up to two orders of magnitudes depending on data source (Wrobel and Reinhardt, 2003). However, the sensitivity analysis revealed that the choice between various emission sets would have moderate influence on human health related impacts (+9 %) and without any influence on the results of ecosystem quality and resource availability impact categories (Table 6).
The assumed PM end-of-life scenario had a strong impact on LCIA results with open-field burning being the worst option in all of the three

damage categories considered. The relatively low increase in impacts in the 100 % on-site burning scenario, as compared to the default scenario, can be explained with the relatively large share of on-site burning option in the default scenario (75%). Combustion of PM in MSWI with energy recovery would reduce the overall human health impacts of cabbage production on average by 21 % as compared to the scenario which as- sumes that all of the PM is burned on-site. In-depth analysis of the results revealed a small (1.5%) advantage of municipal solid waste incinerator (MSWI) with energy recovery over landfilling in all of the three damage categories.

51: Since cabbage has large and lush heads but poorly developed root

system, it requires a lot of nutrients. It is particularly important for the crops to be provided with sufficient amount of nutrients at the beginning of vegetation (Lazic et al., 2001; Guo et al., 2011). Fertilizing with CPM with high content of P2O5 (Table 3) proved to be an excellent measure for improving soil fertility and yields since the soil had medium to very poor content of phosphorus. The experimental field had basic soil re- action (pH > 7) and high content of CaCO3 (5.3 %) (Table 2) which accelerates reaction of Ca and P, and in this case it is recommended to apply phosphorus in excess since it moves quickly into forms that are not readily available for plants (Manojlovié, 2008). In the cases when PM was applied MY from 25.26-52.67 t ha were achieved, which is an increase by 4.11-35.91 % depending on FMs. These yields are slightly lower than those reported by Guo et al. (2011) and Sarker et al. (2003) who used higher fertilizer rates in their experiments, but higher than the
