An ideal temperature for the growth of spring cabbage at the beginning of the vegetation period is 15-18 °C (Cervenski and Medic-Pap, 2018). How- ever, mean daily temperatures over 25 °C are considered unfavourable because they have negative impact on plants, decrease their growth and formation of head, cause loose heads and increase the number of plants that do not form heads (Lazic et al., 2001). The primary reason for lower MY achieved with the application of PM in 2012 (though these differ- ences are not statistically significant, see Fig. 1b) was the considerably higher air temperature (T) during DAT, which was higher by around 2.18 °C compared to the multi-year average (Tmy) (SM 1.2; Table S1). Previous research has also showed that an increase in soil temperature and moisture under plastic mulches and row covers does not always cause an increase in the yield (Gordon et al., 2010). High soil moisture during the entire period of vegetation is necessary to achieve high yield of cabbage and this is the reason why cabbage production is completely oriented to irrigation (Dragovic et al., 2006). Optimal soil moisture should be 80-90% of the field capacity (Nenadov, 1981). The amount of water necessary for cabbage growing depends on pedoclimatic condi- tions and it ranges between 380 and 812 mm (Stanley and Maynard, 1990; Tiwari et al., 2003). The yields achieved and the conditions set up in the experiment can be considered as representative to the regional cabbage production and can serve as a basis for LCA of the environ- mental impact of PM and different FMs.

Results of LCA indicate that irrigation, farming equipment operation, on-field emissions and fertilizer production ranked as top contributors to the life cycle impacts of cabbage production in most of the relevant impact categories. The relatively high share of fertilization and diesel fuel related impacts was expected given the similar observations in previous agricultural LCA studies (Stoessel et al., 2012; Romeiko, 2019). The fairly large environmental impact of irrigation was the consequence

of diesel groundwater pumping and dripline system utilization due to their short lifetime since they have to be replaced annually (Guiso et al., 2015). More surprisingly, the results indicate that production of seed- lings bears a relatively high environmental burden in five out of the seven relevant impact categories contributing from 17 % to 25 % to category indicator results (Fig. 3). In-depth analysis revealed that con- struction, maintenance and disposal of the greenhouse structure is responsible for around half of the impacts associated with seedling production in the HTnc, PMT and TAC categories, whereas peat moss (the growing medium) is the single largest contributor to GW and FD. The carbon footprint and fossil energy requirements of peat moss used in this study were 123 kg CO2 eq and 1404 MJ per m³, respectively (calculated from ecoinvent 3.4 data), which was lower than other esti- mates for peat moss (168-299 kg CO2 eq and 3120-4185 MJ EPAGMA, 2012; or 250 kg CO2 eq Bos et al., 2011), but higher by two orders of magnitude than the carbon footprint and cumulative fossil energy demand of perlite (based on ecoinvent 3.4 data). In previous assessments growing medium had little influence on LCA results of vegetables since seedlings were produced in soil (Payen et al., 2015) or rockwool (Boulard et al., 2011) or because the impacts associated with peat moss production, processing and degradation were not taken into account (Stoessel et al., 2012). Notable reduction in environmental impacts of cabbage production could be achieved by reducing the mass of peat moss per seedling by production in seedling trays (Ponjican et al., 2015), or by using peat moss in combination with other growing me- diums with lower environmental impact such as perlite, vermiculite or sand. However, changing the composition of growing media or substituting one constituent for another may influence the crop quality and yields which should be taken into consideration in LCAs of different growing media (EPAGMA, 2012).

Fertilization increased yields and reduced impacts per MY in each of the three aggregated endpoint categories regardless the fertilization method applied. It is worth noting that compared to other researches (Guo et al., 2011; Sarker et al., 2003) relatively low fertilizer rates were used in this study. The type and amount of fertilizers applied are determined based on the chemical analysis of the soil and fertilizers, and expected yields. Excessive fertilizer use results in negative environ- mental impacts and increase environmental impact of crops (Li et al., 2018).

Clear benefits from PM were observed only in the ecosystem quality category. The ReCiPe 2016 LCIA method measures ecosystem quality as the time integrated loss of local species (Huijbregts et al., 2017) and reduced land occupation and water depletion per unit of MY largely explains the benefits of PM. In the human health and resources avail- ability categories positive effect of PM was determined only in the case of treatment without fertilization (FM1) because of the highest differ- ence in yield (Fig. 1d). Increased impact on resource availability can be explained with the relatively high energy demand of PM production (71 MJ kg Althaus et al., 2007) and a characteristic of the ReCiPe 2016 method which considers only fossil and mineral resources within this damage category (Huijbregts et al., 2017). Combined effect of several impact categories affected by PM resulted in increased damage to human health, with greenhouse gas, particulates and carcinogenic emissions having the highest contribution. These emissions are mainly emitted during the on-site combustion of PM (Wrobel and Reinhardt, 2003).

The three-year average results suggest that when PM is applied lower amount of nutrients are required to achieve comparable yields (Fig. 1d). This has led to reduced N emissions from soil per unit of MY, which in turn decreased the impacts in several midpoint categories. Lower nitrate leaching explains the lower category indicator results in the marine eutrophication impact category (Ngatia et al., 2019), while most of the beneficial influence of PM on stratospheric ozone depletion arises from reduced N2O emissions from soil (Portmann et al., 2012). Emissions of greenhouse gases and particulates from PM disposal (Wrobel and Reinhardt, 2003) and the relatively high energy demand of PM

production (Althaus et al., 2007; Scarascia-Mugnozza et al., 2008) to a large degree offset the advantage of higher MY achieved with PM application which explains the increased category indicator results in GW, PMT and FD impact categories. The adverse impact of mulching on ionizing radiation and some toxicity related impact categories (MET, HTc, HTnc) is largely due to emission of pollutants during the uncon- trolled open field burning of the used PM. Toxicity categories are known to be characterized by high uncertainty because they are mainly deter- mined by plant protection products and metals, which at the time are very case-specific with large variability, and cannot be accurately modelled (Boone et al., 2016).

Results of the sensitivity analyses indicate that the chosen method to allocate the impacts of manure between crops in the cropping cycles has a significant impact on results. Although commonly used in previous LCAs, the no-allocation approach could be discarded since it did not adequately reflect the long-term effect of manure on soil nutrient stocks (Quirós et al., 2015) and does not comply with the ISO 14040:2006 which requires excluding additional functions of the product system investigated (ISO, 2006). The nitrogen- and time-based allocation pro- cedures laid on fundamentally different principles (Quirós et al., 2015) but the choice between them would have only a minor impact on LCIA results. In cases when different allocation procedures are acceptable it is suggested to perform a sensitivity analysis to determine the potential impact of this choice on the LCA results (ISO, 2006).

The results also showed that using thinner PM can reduce the envi- ronmental impact of mulching given the reduced mass of PM applied per unit of area. It should be noted, however, that data on flows associated with the production of PM with different thicknesses were not available; therefore, it was assumed that production of plastic films of different thicknesses bare equal environmental impact on a product mass basis, which is clearly an oversimplification. The great disadvantage of extremely thin PM with thickness of 4-8 um is that it can be easily damaged and difficult to collect, which means that one part remains on the plot (Changrong et al., 2014). The Organic Waste Systems (OWS) company has estimated that up to 68 % of 10 um thick mulching film may remain in the soil (Nessi et al., 2018). The LCA in this study, however, did not consider potential impacts due to the accumulation of plastic in soil (e.g. negative effects of plastic residues on soil quality and potential toxicity-related impacts from residual plastic) due to lack of fundamental fate-exposure-effect data for impact assessment (Nessi et al., 2018). Furthermore, plastic mulches may have negative impact on soil quality by affecting microbial activity which may ultimately affect soil organic matter dynamics (Bandopadhyay et al., 2018), increase soil water repellence and favour the release of greenhouse gases (Steinmetz et al., 2016; Chen et al., 2017). A substantial understanding of these interactions is still lacking (Steinmetz et al., 2016) and given the sig- nificant knowledge gaps it was not possible to quantify these effects in this study.

Results indicate that the assumed PM end-of-life scenario has a strong impact on LCIA results with open-field burning being the worst option in all of the three damage categories considered. The minimum required temperature for complete PE combustion is 750 which is normally achieved only after several minutes under open-field condi- tions (Wrobel and Reinhardt, 2003). Due to the low burning tempera- ture of 400-600 (200-315° or less and incomplete combustion of hydrocarbons, on-site burning of mulch films contaminated with fertil- izers and pesticides usually generates air pollutants, especially dioxins (Levitan and Barro, 2003; Garthe, 2004; Lawrence, 2007). PE film combustion in open field under uncontrolled conditions results in significantly higher emissions of toxic gasses, e.g. dioxin, particulate matter, furans, mercury and polychlorinated biphenyls (Travis and Nixon, 1991; Verma et al., 2016) as compared to high-efficiency in- cinerators capable of burning at 1000-1200 °C or higher which ensures more complete combustion and less pollutant emissions (Garthe, 2004). According to the results of the sensitivity analysis incineration of PM in MSWI with energy recovery has less adverse effects in respect to human

health impacts and resource depletion than landfilling. It is important to note, however, that most power plants and incinerators are not designed to burn dirt and debris covered plastic which in the case of agricultural mulch films can range up to 40-50 % in total mass (Kasirajan and Ngouajio, 2012). Furthermore, in this study the MSWI scenario is modelled base on the optimistic assumptions that the net energy pro- duced can be fully utilized, and that the resulting heat and electricity substitute district or industrial heat from natural gas and electricity from the mainly fossil-based Serbian electricity mix (roughly 70 % of elec- tricity is produced from lignite, while the remaining is generated in hydropower plants; Statistical pocketbook of the Republic of Serbia, 2019). Depending on the case-specific reference electricity and thermal systems and the assumed marketability of the recovered energy the LCA results can vary significantly (Turconi et al., 2011). In this study land- filling was modelled based on data from European sanitary landfills equipped with landfill gas and leachate collection systems (Doka, 2009). However, in many regions of Serbia, dumping of plastic waste into un- controlled dumpsites is still common which poses a serious challenge to environmental quality (Ferronato and Torretta, 2019). Landfilling may be favourable option in areas where the avoided energy systems have low environmental impact and the efficiency of MSWI is low, while incineration may be environmentally justified in countries where the substituted energy systems rely mainly on fossil fuels or the landfills are poorly managed (Eriksson and Finnveden, 2009). In either case proper waste management and legislation are imperative to minimise envi- ronmental impacts. The 7th Environment Action Programme of the EU (Council Decision 1386/2013/EU of 20 November 2013) outlines that by 2020 waste should become a resource that is safely managed, landfill is limited to residual waste (the one that cannot be recycled or reused) and production of energy from waste is limited to non-recyclable raw materials.

In previous assessments growing medium had little influence on LCA results of vegetables since seedlings were produced in soil (Payen et al., 2015) or rockwool (Boulard et al., 2011) or because the impacts associated with peat moss production, processing and degradation were not taken into account (Stoessel et al., 2012). Notable reduction in environmental impacts of cabbage production could be achieved by reducing the mass of peat moss per seedling by production in seedling trays (Ponjican et al., 2015), or by using peat moss in combination with other growing me- diums with lower environmental impact such as perlite, vermiculite or sand. However, changing the composition of growing media or substituting one constituent for another may influence the crop quality and yields which should be taken into consideration in LCAs of different growing media (EPAGMA, 2012).

Fertilization increased yields and reduced impacts per MY in each of the three aggregated endpoint categories regardless the fertilization method applied. It is worth noting that compared to other researches (Guo et al., 2011; Sarker et al., 2003) relatively low fertilizer rates were used in this study. The type and amount of fertilizers applied are determined based on the chemical analysis of the soil and fertilizers, and expected yields. Excessive fertilizer use results in negative environ- mental impacts and increase environmental impact of crops (Li et al., 2018).

Clear benefits from PM were observed only in the ecosystem quality category. The ReCiPe 2016 LCIA method measures ecosystem quality as the time integrated loss of local species (Huijbregts et al., 2017) and reduced land occupation and water depletion per unit of MY largely explains the benefits of PM. In the human health and resources avail- ability categories positive effect of PM was determined only in the case of treatment without fertilization (FM1) because of the highest differ- ence in yield (Fig. 1d). Increased impact on resource availability can be explained with the relatively high energy demand of PM production (71 MJ kg Althaus et al., 2007) and a characteristic of the ReCiPe 2016 method which considers only fossil and mineral resources within this damage category (Huijbregts et al., 2017). Combined effect of several impact categories affected by PM resulted in increased damage to human health, with greenhouse gas, particulates and carcinogenic emissions having the highest contribution. These emissions are mainly emitted during the on-site combustion of PM (Wrobel and Reinhardt, 2003).

The three-year average results suggest that when PM is applied lower amount of nutrients are required to achieve comparable yields (Fig. 1d). This has led to reduced N emissions from soil per unit of MY, which in turn decreased the impacts in several midpoint categories. Lower nitrate leaching explains the lower category indicator results in the marine eutrophication impact category (Ngatia et al., 2019), while most of the beneficial influence of PM on stratospheric ozone depletion arises from reduced N2O emissions from soil (Portmann et al., 2012). Emissions of greenhouse gases and particulates from PM disposal (Wrobel and Reinhardt, 2003) and the relatively high energy demand of PM

production (Althaus et al., 2007; Scarascia-Mugnozza et al., 2008) to a large degree offset the advantage of higher MY achieved with PM application which explains the increased category indicator results in GW, PMT and FD impact categories. The adverse impact of mulching on ionizing radiation and some toxicity related impact categories (MET, HTc, HTnc) is largely due to emission of pollutants during the uncon- trolled open field burning of the used PM. Toxicity categories are known to be characterized by high uncertainty because they are mainly deter- mined by plant protection products and metals, which at the time are very case-specific with large variability, and cannot be accurately modelled (Boone et al., 2016).

Results of the sensitivity analyses indicate that the chosen method to allocate the impacts of manure between crops in the cropping cycles has a significant impact on results. Although commonly used in previous LCAs, the no-allocation approach could be discarded since it did not adequately reflect the long-term effect of manure on soil nutrient stocks (Quirós et al., 2015) and does not comply with the ISO 14040:2006 which requires excluding additional functions of the product system investigated (ISO, 2006). The nitrogen- and time-based allocation pro- cedures laid on fundamentally different principles (Quirós et al., 2015) but the choice between them would have only a minor impact on LCIA results. In cases when different allocation procedures are acceptable it is suggested to perform a sensitivity analysis to determine the potential impact of this choice on the LCA results (ISO, 2006).

The results also showed that using thinner PM can reduce the envi- ronmental impact of mulching given the reduced mass of PM applied per unit of area. It should be noted, however, that data on flows associated with the production of PM with different thicknesses were not available; therefore, it was assumed that production of plastic films of different thicknesses bare equal environmental impact on a product mass basis, which is clearly an oversimplification. The great disadvantage of extremely thin PM with thickness of 4-8 um is that it can be easily damaged and difficult to collect, which means that one part remains on the plot (Changrong et al., 2014). The Organic Waste Systems (OWS) company has estimated that up to 68 % of 10 um thick mulching film may remain in the soil (Nessi et al., 2018). The LCA in this study, however, did not consider potential impacts due to the accumulation of plastic in soil (e.g. negative effects of plastic residues on soil quality and potential toxicity-related impacts from residual plastic) due to lack of fundamental fate-exposure-effect data for impact assessment (Nessi et al., 2018). Furthermore, plastic mulches may have negative impact on soil quality by affecting microbial activity which may ultimately affect soil organic matter dynamics (Bandopadhyay et al., 2018), increase soil water repellence and favour the release of greenhouse gases (Steinmetz et al., 2016; Chen et al., 2017). A substantial understanding of these interactions is still lacking (Steinmetz et al., 2016) and given the sig- nificant knowledge gaps it was not possible to quantify these effects in this study.

Results indicate that the assumed PM end-of-life scenario has a strong impact on LCIA results with open-field burning being the worst option in all of the three damage categories considered. The minimum required temperature for complete PE combustion is 750 which is normally achieved only after several minutes under open-field condi- tions (Wrobel and Reinhardt, 2003). Due to the low burning tempera- ture of 400-600 (200-315° or less and incomplete combustion of hydrocarbons, on-site burning of mulch films contaminated with fertil- izers and pesticides usually generates air pollutants, especially dioxins (Levitan and Barro, 2003; Garthe, 2004; Lawrence, 2007). PE film combustion in open field under uncontrolled conditions results in significantly higher emissions of toxic gasses, e.g. dioxin, particulate matter, furans, mercury and polychlorinated biphenyls (Travis and Nixon, 1991; Verma et al., 2016) as compared to high-efficiency in- cinerators capable of burning at 1000-1200 °C or higher which ensures more complete combustion and less pollutant emissions (Garthe, 2004). According to the results of the sensitivity analysis incineration of PM in MSWI with energy recovery has less adverse effects in respect to human

health impacts and resource depletion than landfilling. It is important to note, however, that most power plants and incinerators are not designed to burn dirt and debris covered plastic which in the case of agricultural mulch films can range up to 40-50 % in total mass (Kasirajan and Ngouajio, 2012). Furthermore, in this study the MSWI scenario is modelled base on the optimistic assumptions that the net energy pro- duced can be fully utilized, and that the resulting heat and electricity substitute district or industrial heat from natural gas and electricity from the mainly fossil-based Serbian electricity mix (roughly 70 % of elec- tricity is produced from lignite, while the remaining is generated in hydropower plants; Statistical pocketbook of the Republic of Serbia, 2019). Depending on the case-specific reference electricity and thermal systems and the assumed marketability of the recovered energy the LCA results can vary significantly (Turconi et al., 2011). In this study land- filling was modelled based on data from European sanitary landfills equipped with landfill gas and leachate collection systems (Doka, 2009). However, in many regions of Serbia, dumping of plastic waste into un- controlled dumpsites is still common which poses a serious challenge to environmental quality (Ferronato and Torretta, 2019). Landfilling may be favourable option in areas where the avoided energy systems have low environmental impact and the efficiency of MSWI is low, while incineration may be environmentally justified in countries where the substituted energy systems rely mainly on fossil fuels or the landfills are poorly managed (Eriksson and Finnveden, 2009). In either case proper waste management and legislation are imperative to minimise envi- ronmental impacts. The 7th Environment Action Programme of the EU (Council Decision 1386/2013/EU of 20 November 2013) outlines that by 2020 waste should become a resource that is safely managed, landfill is limited to residual waste (the one that cannot be recycled or reused) and production of energy from waste is limited to non-recyclable raw materials.
65: Under conditions set up in the experiment, fertilizing had positive and statistically significant effect on yields of spring cabbage. Regardless of the fertilization method applied, increased yields due to fertilization completely offset the increased damage caused by the application of fertilizers. Depending on the fertilization method and damage category considered, fertilization reduced damage by 18-44 per unit of cab- bage marketable yield (MY) in cases where plastic mulch (PM) was not applied. PM has in general favorable and statistical significant impact on yields; however, the benefits from PM are more pronounced in cases where fertilizers are not applied, and under certain fertilization schemes mulching had only a modest influence on yields. In fact, PM reduced by a third or even more than half the environmental gains achieved with fertilizations.

Based on the three-year average results it can be stated that PM is likely to reduce environmental impacts per unit of MY in 12 out of 18 midpoint categories considered by the ReCiPe 2016 method. The posi- tive environmental impact of PM application resulted from lower con- sumption of irrigation water, lower fuel (diesel) consumption, lower nitrogen emissions from soil and lower machinery use per unit of MY achieved; and negative impact was mainly the consequence of the pro- duction and end-of-life treatment of PM. In the three aggregated endpoint categories clear environmental benefits from PM were deter- mined only in the ecosystem quality category mainly due to reduced water and land requirements. Considering human health and resources availability indicators, the positive effect of mulching was determined only in the case of treatment without fertilization because of the highest difference in MY. Although on-site burning is one of the most common and technically most readily applied end-of-life PM treatment, it is also the most unacceptable solution from the environmental aspect. Land- filling or incineration of PM in MSWI with energy recovery would reduce the environmental impacts of cabbage production on human health by 16-21% respectively, as compared to the scenario which
assumes that PM is burned on-site. The experiment was conducted with

66: Under conditions set up in the experiment, fertilizing had positive and statistically significant effect on yields of spring cabbage. Regardless of the fertilization method applied, increased yields due to fertilization completely offset the increased damage caused by the application of fertilizers. Depending on the fertilization method and damage category considered, fertilization reduced damage by 18-44 per unit of cab- bage marketable yield (MY) in cases where plastic mulch (PM) was not applied. PM has in general favorable and statistical significant impact on yields; however, the benefits from PM are more pronounced in cases where fertilizers are not applied, and under certain fertilization schemes mulching had only a modest influence on yields. In fact, PM reduced by a third or even more than half the environmental gains achieved with fertilizations.

Based on the three-year average results it can be stated that PM is likely to reduce environmental impacts per unit of MY in 12 out of 18 midpoint categories considered by the ReCiPe 2016 method. The posi- tive environmental impact of PM application resulted from lower con- sumption of irrigation water, lower fuel (diesel) consumption, lower nitrogen emissions from soil and lower machinery use per unit of MY achieved; and negative impact was mainly the consequence of the pro- duction and end-of-life treatment of PM. In the three aggregated endpoint categories clear environmental benefits from PM were deter- mined only in the ecosystem quality category mainly due to reduced water and land requirements. Considering human health and resources availability indicators, the positive effect of mulching was determined only in the case of treatment without fertilization because of the highest difference in MY. Although on-site burning is one of the most common and technically most readily applied end-of-life PM treatment, it is also the most unacceptable solution from the environmental aspect. Land- filling or incineration of PM in MSWI with energy recovery would reduce the environmental impacts of cabbage production on human health by 16-21% respectively, as compared to the scenario which
assumes that PM is burned on-site. The experiment was conducted with

67: Under conditions set up in the experiment, fertilizing had positive and statistically significant effect on yields of spring cabbage. Regardless of the fertilization method applied, increased yields due to fertilization completely offset the increased damage caused by the application of fertilizers. Depending on the fertilization method and damage category considered, fertilization reduced damage by 18-44 per unit of cab- bage marketable yield (MY) in cases where plastic mulch (PM) was not applied. PM has in general favorable and statistical significant impact on yields; however, the benefits from PM are more pronounced in cases where fertilizers are not applied, and under certain fertilization schemes mulching had only a modest influence on yields. In fact, PM reduced by a third or even more than half the environmental gains achieved with fertilizations.

Based on the three-year average results it can be stated that PM is likely to reduce environmental impacts per unit of MY in 12 out of 18 midpoint categories considered by the ReCiPe 2016 method. The posi- tive environmental impact of PM application resulted from lower con- sumption of irrigation water, lower fuel (diesel) consumption, lower nitrogen emissions from soil and lower machinery use per unit of MY achieved; and negative impact was mainly the consequence of the pro- duction and end-of-life treatment of PM. In the three aggregated endpoint categories clear environmental benefits from PM were deter- mined only in the ecosystem quality category mainly due to reduced water and land requirements. Considering human health and resources availability indicators, the positive effect of mulching was determined only in the case of treatment without fertilization because of the highest difference in MY. Although on-site burning is one of the most common and technically most readily applied end-of-life PM treatment, it is also the most unacceptable solution from the environmental aspect. Land- filling or incineration of PM in MSWI with energy recovery would reduce the environmental impacts of cabbage production on human health by 16-21% respectively, as compared to the scenario which
assumes that PM is burned on-site. The experiment was conducted with

68: Under conditions set up in the experiment, fertilizing had positive and statistically significant effect on yields of spring cabbage. Regardless of the fertilization method applied, increased yields due to fertilization completely offset the increased damage caused by the application of fertilizers. Depending on the fertilization method and damage category considered, fertilization reduced damage by 18-44 per unit of cab- bage marketable yield (MY) in cases where plastic mulch (PM) was not applied. PM has in general favorable and statistical significant impact on yields; however, the benefits from PM are more pronounced in cases where fertilizers are not applied, and under certain fertilization schemes mulching had only a modest influence on yields. In fact, PM reduced by a third or even more than half the environmental gains achieved with fertilizations.

Based on the three-year average results it can be stated that PM is likely to reduce environmental impacts per unit of MY in 12 out of 18 midpoint categories considered by the ReCiPe 2016 method. The posi- tive environmental impact of PM application resulted from lower con- sumption of irrigation water, lower fuel (diesel) consumption, lower nitrogen emissions from soil and lower machinery use per unit of MY achieved; and negative impact was mainly the consequence of the pro- duction and end-of-life treatment of PM. In the three aggregated endpoint categories clear environmental benefits from PM were deter- mined only in the ecosystem quality category mainly due to reduced water and land requirements. Considering human health and resources availability indicators, the positive effect of mulching was determined only in the case of treatment without fertilization because of the highest difference in MY. Although on-site burning is one of the most common and technically most readily applied end-of-life PM treatment, it is also the most unacceptable solution from the environmental aspect. Land- filling or incineration of PM in MSWI with energy recovery would reduce the environmental impacts of cabbage production on human health by 16-21% respectively, as compared to the scenario which
assumes that PM is burned on-site. The experiment was conducted with

69: Under conditions set up in the experiment, fertilizing had positive and statistically significant effect on yields of spring cabbage. Regardless of the fertilization method applied, increased yields due to fertilization completely offset the increased damage caused by the application of fertilizers. Depending on the fertilization method and damage category considered, fertilization reduced damage by 18-44 per unit of cab- bage marketable yield (MY) in cases where plastic mulch (PM) was not applied. PM has in general favorable and statistical significant impact on yields; however, the benefits from PM are more pronounced in cases where fertilizers are not applied, and under certain fertilization schemes mulching had only a modest influence on yields. In fact, PM reduced by a third or even more than half the environmental gains achieved with fertilizations.

Based on the three-year average results it can be stated that PM is likely to reduce environmental impacts per unit of MY in 12 out of 18 midpoint categories considered by the ReCiPe 2016 method. The posi- tive environmental impact of PM application resulted from lower con- sumption of irrigation water, lower fuel (diesel) consumption, lower nitrogen emissions from soil and lower machinery use per unit of MY achieved; and negative impact was mainly the consequence of the pro- duction and end-of-life treatment of PM. In the three aggregated endpoint categories clear environmental benefits from PM were deter- mined only in the ecosystem quality category mainly due to reduced water and land requirements. Considering human health and resources availability indicators, the positive effect of mulching was determined only in the case of treatment without fertilization because of the highest difference in MY. Although on-site burning is one of the most common and technically most readily applied end-of-life PM treatment, it is also the most unacceptable solution from the environmental aspect. Land- filling or incineration of PM in MSWI with energy recovery would reduce the environmental impacts of cabbage production on human health by 16-21% respectively, as compared to the scenario which
assumes that PM is burned on-site. The experiment was conducted with

70: Under conditions set up in the experiment, fertilizing had positive and statistically significant effect on yields of spring cabbage. Regardless of the fertilization method applied, increased yields due to fertilization completely offset the increased damage caused by the application of fertilizers. Depending on the fertilization method and damage category considered, fertilization reduced damage by 18-44 per unit of cab- bage marketable yield (MY) in cases where plastic mulch (PM) was not applied. PM has in general favorable and statistical significant impact on yields; however, the benefits from PM are more pronounced in cases where fertilizers are not applied, and under certain fertilization schemes mulching had only a modest influence on yields. In fact, PM reduced by a third or even more than half the environmental gains achieved with fertilizations.

Based on the three-year average results it can be stated that PM is likely to reduce environmental impacts per unit of MY in 12 out of 18 midpoint categories considered by the ReCiPe 2016 method. The posi- tive environmental impact of PM application resulted from lower con- sumption of irrigation water, lower fuel (diesel) consumption, lower nitrogen emissions from soil and lower machinery use per unit of MY achieved; and negative impact was mainly the consequence of the pro- duction and end-of-life treatment of PM. In the three aggregated endpoint categories clear environmental benefits from PM were deter- mined only in the ecosystem quality category mainly due to reduced water and land requirements. Considering human health and resources availability indicators, the positive effect of mulching was determined only in the case of treatment without fertilization because of the highest difference in MY. Although on-site burning is one of the most common and technically most readily applied end-of-life PM treatment, it is also the most unacceptable solution from the environmental aspect. Land- filling or incineration of PM in MSWI with energy recovery would reduce the environmental impacts of cabbage production on human health by 16-21% respectively, as compared to the scenario which
assumes that PM is burned on-site. The experiment was conducted with

71: Under conditions set up in the experiment, fertilizing had positive and statistically significant effect on yields of spring cabbage. Regardless of the fertilization method applied, increased yields due to fertilization completely offset the increased damage caused by the application of fertilizers. Depending on the fertilization method and damage category considered, fertilization reduced damage by 18-44 per unit of cab- bage marketable yield (MY) in cases where plastic mulch (PM) was not applied. PM has in general favorable and statistical significant impact on yields; however, the benefits from PM are more pronounced in cases where fertilizers are not applied, and under certain fertilization schemes mulching had only a modest influence on yields. In fact, PM reduced by a third or even more than half the environmental gains achieved with fertilizations.

Based on the three-year average results it can be stated that PM is likely to reduce environmental impacts per unit of MY in 12 out of 18 midpoint categories considered by the ReCiPe 2016 method. The posi- tive environmental impact of PM application resulted from lower con- sumption of irrigation water, lower fuel (diesel) consumption, lower nitrogen emissions from soil and lower machinery use per unit of MY achieved; and negative impact was mainly the consequence of the pro- duction and end-of-life treatment of PM. In the three aggregated endpoint categories clear environmental benefits from PM were deter- mined only in the ecosystem quality category mainly due to reduced water and land requirements. Considering human health and resources availability indicators, the positive effect of mulching was determined only in the case of treatment without fertilization because of the highest difference in MY. Although on-site burning is one of the most common and technically most readily applied end-of-life PM treatment, it is also the most unacceptable solution from the environmental aspect. Land- filling or incineration of PM in MSWI with energy recovery would reduce the environmental impacts of cabbage production on human health by 16-21% respectively, as compared to the scenario which
assumes that PM is burned on-site. The experiment was conducted with

72: Under conditions set up in the experiment, fertilizing had positive and statistically significant effect on yields of spring cabbage. Regardless of the fertilization method applied, increased yields due to fertilization completely offset the increased damage caused by the application of fertilizers. Depending on the fertilization method and damage category considered, fertilization reduced damage by 18-44 per unit of cab- bage marketable yield (MY) in cases where plastic mulch (PM) was not applied. PM has in general favorable and statistical significant impact on yields; however, the benefits from PM are more pronounced in cases where fertilizers are not applied, and under certain fertilization schemes mulching had only a modest influence on yields. In fact, PM reduced by a third or even more than half the environmental gains achieved with fertilizations.

Based on the three-year average results it can be stated that PM is likely to reduce environmental impacts per unit of MY in 12 out of 18 midpoint categories considered by the ReCiPe 2016 method. The posi- tive environmental impact of PM application resulted from lower con- sumption of irrigation water, lower fuel (diesel) consumption, lower nitrogen emissions from soil and lower machinery use per unit of MY achieved; and negative impact was mainly the consequence of the pro- duction and end-of-life treatment of PM. In the three aggregated endpoint categories clear environmental benefits from PM were deter- mined only in the ecosystem quality category mainly due to reduced water and land requirements. Considering human health and resources availability indicators, the positive effect of mulching was determined only in the case of treatment without fertilization because of the highest difference in MY. Although on-site burning is one of the most common and technically most readily applied end-of-life PM treatment, it is also the most unacceptable solution from the environmental aspect. Land- filling or incineration of PM in MSWI with energy recovery would reduce the environmental impacts of cabbage production on human health by 16-21% respectively, as compared to the scenario which
assumes that PM is burned on-site. The experiment was conducted with

73: Under conditions set up in the experiment, fertilizing had positive and statistically significant effect on yields of spring cabbage. Regardless of the fertilization method applied, increased yields due to fertilization completely offset the increased damage caused by the application of fertilizers. Depending on the fertilization method and damage category considered, fertilization reduced damage by 18-44 per unit of cab- bage marketable yield (MY) in cases where plastic mulch (PM) was not applied. PM has in general favorable and statistical significant impact on yields; however, the benefits from PM are more pronounced in cases where fertilizers are not applied, and under certain fertilization schemes mulching had only a modest influence on yields. In fact, PM reduced by a third or even more than half the environmental gains achieved with fertilizations.

Based on the three-year average results it can be stated that PM is likely to reduce environmental impacts per unit of MY in 12 out of 18 midpoint categories considered by the ReCiPe 2016 method. The posi- tive environmental impact of PM application resulted from lower con- sumption of irrigation water, lower fuel (diesel) consumption, lower nitrogen emissions from soil and lower machinery use per unit of MY achieved; and negative impact was mainly the consequence of the pro- duction and end-of-life treatment of PM. In the three aggregated endpoint categories clear environmental benefits from PM were deter- mined only in the ecosystem quality category mainly due to reduced water and land requirements. Considering human health and resources availability indicators, the positive effect of mulching was determined only in the case of treatment without fertilization because of the highest difference in MY. Although on-site burning is one of the most common and technically most readily applied end-of-life PM treatment, it is also the most unacceptable solution from the environmental aspect. Land- filling or incineration of PM in MSWI with energy recovery would reduce the environmental impacts of cabbage production on human health by 16-21% respectively, as compared to the scenario which
assumes that PM is burned on-site. The experiment was conducted with

74: Under conditions set up in the experiment, fertilizing had positive and statistically significant effect on yields of spring cabbage. Regardless of the fertilization method applied, increased yields due to fertilization completely offset the increased damage caused by the application of fertilizers. Depending on the fertilization method and damage category considered, fertilization reduced damage by 18-44 per unit of cab- bage marketable yield (MY) in cases where plastic mulch (PM) was not applied. PM has in general favorable and statistical significant impact on yields; however, the benefits from PM are more pronounced in cases where fertilizers are not applied, and under certain fertilization schemes mulching had only a modest influence on yields. In fact, PM reduced by a third or even more than half the environmental gains achieved with fertilizations.

Based on the three-year average results it can be stated that PM is likely to reduce environmental impacts per unit of MY in 12 out of 18 midpoint categories considered by the ReCiPe 2016 method. The posi- tive environmental impact of PM application resulted from lower con- sumption of irrigation water, lower fuel (diesel) consumption, lower nitrogen emissions from soil and lower machinery use per unit of MY achieved; and negative impact was mainly the consequence of the pro- duction and end-of-life treatment of PM. In the three aggregated endpoint categories clear environmental benefits from PM were deter- mined only in the ecosystem quality category mainly due to reduced water and land requirements. Considering human health and resources availability indicators, the positive effect of mulching was determined only in the case of treatment without fertilization because of the highest difference in MY. Although on-site burning is one of the most common and technically most readily applied end-of-life PM treatment, it is also the most unacceptable solution from the environmental aspect. Land- filling or incineration of PM in MSWI with energy recovery would reduce the environmental impacts of cabbage production on human health by 16-21% respectively, as compared to the scenario which
assumes that PM is burned on-site. The experiment was conducted with

75: Under conditions set up in the experiment, fertilizing had positive and statistically significant effect on yields of spring cabbage. Regardless of the fertilization method applied, increased yields due to fertilization completely offset the increased damage caused by the application of fertilizers. Depending on the fertilization method and damage category considered, fertilization reduced damage by 18-44 per unit of cab- bage marketable yield (MY) in cases where plastic mulch (PM) was not applied. PM has in general favorable and statistical significant impact on yields; however, the benefits from PM are more pronounced in cases where fertilizers are not applied, and under certain fertilization schemes mulching had only a modest influence on yields. In fact, PM reduced by a third or even more than half the environmental gains achieved with fertilizations.

Based on the three-year average results it can be stated that PM is likely to reduce environmental impacts per unit of MY in 12 out of 18 midpoint categories considered by the ReCiPe 2016 method. The posi- tive environmental impact of PM application resulted from lower con- sumption of irrigation water, lower fuel (diesel) consumption, lower nitrogen emissions from soil and lower machinery use per unit of MY achieved; and negative impact was mainly the consequence of the pro- duction and end-of-life treatment of PM. In the three aggregated endpoint categories clear environmental benefits from PM were deter- mined only in the ecosystem quality category mainly due to reduced water and land requirements. Considering human health and resources availability indicators, the positive effect of mulching was determined only in the case of treatment without fertilization because of the highest difference in MY. Although on-site burning is one of the most common and technically most readily applied end-of-life PM treatment, it is also the most unacceptable solution from the environmental aspect. Land- filling or incineration of PM in MSWI with energy recovery would reduce the environmental impacts of cabbage production on human health by 16-21% respectively, as compared to the scenario which
assumes that PM is burned on-site. The experiment was conducted with

76: Under conditions set up in the experiment, fertilizing had positive and statistically significant effect on yields of spring cabbage. Regardless of the fertilization method applied, increased yields due to fertilization completely offset the increased damage caused by the application of fertilizers. Depending on the fertilization method and damage category considered, fertilization reduced damage by 18-44 per unit of cab- bage marketable yield (MY) in cases where plastic mulch (PM) was not applied. PM has in general favorable and statistical significant impact on yields; however, the benefits from PM are more pronounced in cases where fertilizers are not applied, and under certain fertilization schemes mulching had only a modest influence on yields. In fact, PM reduced by a third or even more than half the environmental gains achieved with fertilizations.

Based on the three-year average results it can be stated that PM is likely to reduce environmental impacts per unit of MY in 12 out of 18 midpoint categories considered by the ReCiPe 2016 method. The posi- tive environmental impact of PM application resulted from lower con- sumption of irrigation water, lower fuel (diesel) consumption, lower nitrogen emissions from soil and lower machinery use per unit of MY achieved; and negative impact was mainly the consequence of the pro- duction and end-of-life treatment of PM. In the three aggregated endpoint categories clear environmental benefits from PM were deter- mined only in the ecosystem quality category mainly due to reduced water and land requirements. Considering human health and resources availability indicators, the positive effect of mulching was determined only in the case of treatment without fertilization because of the highest difference in MY. Although on-site burning is one of the most common and technically most readily applied end-of-life PM treatment, it is also the most unacceptable solution from the environmental aspect. Land- filling or incineration of PM in MSWI with energy recovery would reduce the environmental impacts of cabbage production on human health by 16-21% respectively, as compared to the scenario which
assumes that PM is burned on-site. The experiment was conducted with

77: Under conditions set up in the experiment, fertilizing had positive and statistically significant effect on yields of spring cabbage. Regardless of the fertilization method applied, increased yields due to fertilization completely offset the increased damage caused by the application of fertilizers. Depending on the fertilization method and damage category considered, fertilization reduced damage by 18-44 per unit of cab- bage marketable yield (MY) in cases where plastic mulch (PM) was not applied. PM has in general favorable and statistical significant impact on yields; however, the benefits from PM are more pronounced in cases where fertilizers are not applied, and under certain fertilization schemes mulching had only a modest influence on yields. In fact, PM reduced by a third or even more than half the environmental gains achieved with fertilizations.

Based on the three-year average results it can be stated that PM is likely to reduce environmental impacts per unit of MY in 12 out of 18 midpoint categories considered by the ReCiPe 2016 method. The posi- tive environmental impact of PM application resulted from lower con- sumption of irrigation water, lower fuel (diesel) consumption, lower nitrogen emissions from soil and lower machinery use per unit of MY achieved; and negative impact was mainly the consequence of the pro- duction and end-of-life treatment of PM. In the three aggregated endpoint categories clear environmental benefits from PM were deter- mined only in the ecosystem quality category mainly due to reduced water and land requirements. Considering human health and resources availability indicators, the positive effect of mulching was determined only in the case of treatment without fertilization because of the highest difference in MY. Although on-site burning is one of the most common and technically most readily applied end-of-life PM treatment, it is also the most unacceptable solution from the environmental aspect. Land- filling or incineration of PM in MSWI with energy recovery would reduce the environmental impacts of cabbage production on human health by 16-21% respectively, as compared to the scenario which
assumes that PM is burned on-site. The experiment was conducted with

78: Under conditions set up in the experiment, fertilizing had positive and statistically significant effect on yields of spring cabbage. Regardless of the fertilization method applied, increased yields due to fertilization completely offset the increased damage caused by the application of fertilizers. Depending on the fertilization method and damage category considered, fertilization reduced damage by 18-44 per unit of cab- bage marketable yield (MY) in cases where plastic mulch (PM) was not applied. PM has in general favorable and statistical significant impact on yields; however, the benefits from PM are more pronounced in cases where fertilizers are not applied, and under certain fertilization schemes mulching had only a modest influence on yields. In fact, PM reduced by a third or even more than half the environmental gains achieved with fertilizations.

Based on the three-year average results it can be stated that PM is likely to reduce environmental impacts per unit of MY in 12 out of 18 midpoint categories considered by the ReCiPe 2016 method. The posi- tive environmental impact of PM application resulted from lower con- sumption of irrigation water, lower fuel (diesel) consumption, lower nitrogen emissions from soil and lower machinery use per unit of MY achieved; and negative impact was mainly the consequence of the pro- duction and end-of-life treatment of PM. In the three aggregated endpoint categories clear environmental benefits from PM were deter- mined only in the ecosystem quality category mainly due to reduced water and land requirements. Considering human health and resources availability indicators, the positive effect of mulching was determined only in the case of treatment without fertilization because of the highest difference in MY. Although on-site burning is one of the most common and technically most readily applied end-of-life PM treatment, it is also the most unacceptable solution from the environmental aspect. Land- filling or incineration of PM in MSWI with energy recovery would reduce the environmental impacts of cabbage production on human health by 16-21% respectively, as compared to the scenario which
assumes that PM is burned on-site. The experiment was conducted with

79: Under conditions set up in the experiment, fertilizing had positive and statistically significant effect on yields of spring cabbage. Regardless of the fertilization method applied, increased yields due to fertilization completely offset the increased damage caused by the application of fertilizers. Depending on the fertilization method and damage category considered, fertilization reduced damage by 18-44 per unit of cab- bage marketable yield (MY) in cases where plastic mulch (PM) was not applied. PM has in general favorable and statistical significant impact on yields; however, the benefits from PM are more pronounced in cases where fertilizers are not applied, and under certain fertilization schemes mulching had only a modest influence on yields. In fact, PM reduced by a third or even more than half the environmental gains achieved with fertilizations.

Based on the three-year average results it can be stated that PM is likely to reduce environmental impacts per unit of MY in 12 out of 18 midpoint categories considered by the ReCiPe 2016 method. The posi- tive environmental impact of PM application resulted from lower con- sumption of irrigation water, lower fuel (diesel) consumption, lower nitrogen emissions from soil and lower machinery use per unit of MY achieved; and negative impact was mainly the consequence of the pro- duction and end-of-life treatment of PM. In the three aggregated endpoint categories clear environmental benefits from PM were deter- mined only in the ecosystem quality category mainly due to reduced water and land requirements. Considering human health and resources availability indicators, the positive effect of mulching was determined only in the case of treatment without fertilization because of the highest difference in MY. Although on-site burning is one of the most common and technically most readily applied end-of-life PM treatment, it is also the most unacceptable solution from the environmental aspect. Land- filling or incineration of PM in MSWI with energy recovery would reduce the environmental impacts of cabbage production on human health by 16-21% respectively, as compared to the scenario which
assumes that PM is burned on-site. The experiment was conducted with

80: Under conditions set up in the experiment, fertilizing had positive and statistically significant effect on yields of spring cabbage. Regardless of the fertilization method applied, increased yields due to fertilization completely offset the increased damage caused by the application of fertilizers. Depending on the fertilization method and damage category considered, fertilization reduced damage by 18-44 per unit of cab- bage marketable yield (MY) in cases where plastic mulch (PM) was not applied. PM has in general favorable and statistical significant impact on yields; however, the benefits from PM are more pronounced in cases where fertilizers are not applied, and under certain fertilization schemes mulching had only a modest influence on yields. In fact, PM reduced by a third or even more than half the environmental gains achieved with fertilizations.

Based on the three-year average results it can be stated that PM is likely to reduce environmental impacts per unit of MY in 12 out of 18 midpoint categories considered by the ReCiPe 2016 method. The posi- tive environmental impact of PM application resulted from lower con- sumption of irrigation water, lower fuel (diesel) consumption, lower nitrogen emissions from soil and lower machinery use per unit of MY achieved; and negative impact was mainly the consequence of the pro- duction and end-of-life treatment of PM. In the three aggregated endpoint categories clear environmental benefits from PM were deter- mined only in the ecosystem quality category mainly due to reduced water and land requirements. Considering human health and resources availability indicators, the positive effect of mulching was determined only in the case of treatment without fertilization because of the highest difference in MY. Although on-site burning is one of the most common and technically most readily applied end-of-life PM treatment, it is also the most unacceptable solution from the environmental aspect. Land- filling or incineration of PM in MSWI with energy recovery would reduce the environmental impacts of cabbage production on human health by 16-21% respectively, as compared to the scenario which
assumes that PM is burned on-site. The experiment was conducted with

81: Under conditions set up in the experiment, fertilizing had positive and statistically significant effect on yields of spring cabbage. Regardless of the fertilization method applied, increased yields due to fertilization completely offset the increased damage caused by the application of fertilizers. Depending on the fertilization method and damage category considered, fertilization reduced damage by 18-44 per unit of cab- bage marketable yield (MY) in cases where plastic mulch (PM) was not applied. PM has in general favorable and statistical significant impact on yields; however, the benefits from PM are more pronounced in cases where fertilizers are not applied, and under certain fertilization schemes mulching had only a modest influence on yields. In fact, PM reduced by a third or even more than half the environmental gains achieved with fertilizations.

Based on the three-year average results it can be stated that PM is likely to reduce environmental impacts per unit of MY in 12 out of 18 midpoint categories considered by the ReCiPe 2016 method. The posi- tive environmental impact of PM application resulted from lower con- sumption of irrigation water, lower fuel (diesel) consumption, lower nitrogen emissions from soil and lower machinery use per unit of MY achieved; and negative impact was mainly the consequence of the pro- duction and end-of-life treatment of PM. In the three aggregated endpoint categories clear environmental benefits from PM were deter- mined only in the ecosystem quality category mainly due to reduced water and land requirements. Considering human health and resources availability indicators, the positive effect of mulching was determined only in the case of treatment without fertilization because of the highest difference in MY. Although on-site burning is one of the most common and technically most readily applied end-of-life PM treatment, it is also the most unacceptable solution from the environmental aspect. Land- filling or incineration of PM in MSWI with energy recovery would reduce the environmental impacts of cabbage production on human health by 16-21% respectively, as compared to the scenario which
assumes that PM is burned on-site. The experiment was conducted with

82: Under conditions set up in the experiment, fertilizing had positive and statistically significant effect on yields of spring cabbage. Regardless of the fertilization method applied, increased yields due to fertilization completely offset the increased damage caused by the application of fertilizers. Depending on the fertilization method and damage category considered, fertilization reduced damage by 18-44 per unit of cab- bage marketable yield (MY) in cases where plastic mulch (PM) was not applied. PM has in general favorable and statistical significant impact on yields; however, the benefits from PM are more pronounced in cases where fertilizers are not applied, and under certain fertilization schemes mulching had only a modest influence on yields. In fact, PM reduced by a third or even more than half the environmental gains achieved with fertilizations.

Based on the three-year average results it can be stated that PM is likely to reduce environmental impacts per unit of MY in 12 out of 18 midpoint categories considered by the ReCiPe 2016 method. The posi- tive environmental impact of PM application resulted from lower con- sumption of irrigation water, lower fuel (diesel) consumption, lower nitrogen emissions from soil and lower machinery use per unit of MY achieved; and negative impact was mainly the consequence of the pro- duction and end-of-life treatment of PM. In the three aggregated endpoint categories clear environmental benefits from PM were deter- mined only in the ecosystem quality category mainly due to reduced water and land requirements. Considering human health and resources availability indicators, the positive effect of mulching was determined only in the case of treatment without fertilization because of the highest difference in MY. Although on-site burning is one of the most common and technically most readily applied end-of-life PM treatment, it is also the most unacceptable solution from the environmental aspect. Land- filling or incineration of PM in MSWI with energy recovery would reduce the environmental impacts of cabbage production on human health by 16-21% respectively, as compared to the scenario which
assumes that PM is burned on-site. The experiment was conducted with

83: Under conditions set up in the experiment, fertilizing had positive and statistically significant effect on yields of spring cabbage. Regardless of the fertilization method applied, increased yields due to fertilization completely offset the increased damage caused by the application of fertilizers. Depending on the fertilization method and damage category considered, fertilization reduced damage by 18-44 per unit of cab- bage marketable yield (MY) in cases where plastic mulch (PM) was not applied. PM has in general favorable and statistical significant impact on yields; however, the benefits from PM are more pronounced in cases where fertilizers are not applied, and under certain fertilization schemes mulching had only a modest influence on yields. In fact, PM reduced by a third or even more than half the environmental gains achieved with fertilizations.

Based on the three-year average results it can be stated that PM is likely to reduce environmental impacts per unit of MY in 12 out of 18 midpoint categories considered by the ReCiPe 2016 method. The posi- tive environmental impact of PM application resulted from lower con- sumption of irrigation water, lower fuel (diesel) consumption, lower nitrogen emissions from soil and lower machinery use per unit of MY achieved; and negative impact was mainly the consequence of the pro- duction and end-of-life treatment of PM. In the three aggregated endpoint categories clear environmental benefits from PM were deter- mined only in the ecosystem quality category mainly due to reduced water and land requirements. Considering human health and resources availability indicators, the positive effect of mulching was determined only in the case of treatment without fertilization because of the highest difference in MY. Although on-site burning is one of the most common and technically most readily applied end-of-life PM treatment, it is also the most unacceptable solution from the environmental aspect. Land- filling or incineration of PM in MSWI with energy recovery would reduce the environmental impacts of cabbage production on human health by 16-21% respectively, as compared to the scenario which
assumes that PM is burned on-site. The experiment was conducted with

84: Under conditions set up in the experiment, fertilizing had positive and statistically significant effect on yields of spring cabbage. Regardless of the fertilization method applied, increased yields due to fertilization completely offset the increased damage caused by the application of fertilizers. Depending on the fertilization method and damage category considered, fertilization reduced damage by 18-44 per unit of cab- bage marketable yield (MY) in cases where plastic mulch (PM) was not applied. PM has in general favorable and statistical significant impact on yields; however, the benefits from PM are more pronounced in cases where fertilizers are not applied, and under certain fertilization schemes mulching had only a modest influence on yields. In fact, PM reduced by a third or even more than half the environmental gains achieved with fertilizations.

Based on the three-year average results it can be stated that PM is likely to reduce environmental impacts per unit of MY in 12 out of 18 midpoint categories considered by the ReCiPe 2016 method. The posi- tive environmental impact of PM application resulted from lower con- sumption of irrigation water, lower fuel (diesel) consumption, lower nitrogen emissions from soil and lower machinery use per unit of MY achieved; and negative impact was mainly the consequence of the pro- duction and end-of-life treatment of PM. In the three aggregated endpoint categories clear environmental benefits from PM were deter- mined only in the ecosystem quality category mainly due to reduced water and land requirements. Considering human health and resources availability indicators, the positive effect of mulching was determined only in the case of treatment without fertilization because of the highest difference in MY. Although on-site burning is one of the most common and technically most readily applied end-of-life PM treatment, it is also the most unacceptable solution from the environmental aspect. Land- filling or incineration of PM in MSWI with energy recovery would reduce the environmental impacts of cabbage production on human health by 16-21% respectively, as compared to the scenario which
assumes that PM is burned on-site. The experiment was conducted with

85: Under conditions set up in the experiment, fertilizing had positive and statistically significant effect on yields of spring cabbage. Regardless of the fertilization method applied, increased yields due to fertilization completely offset the increased damage caused by the application of fertilizers. Depending on the fertilization method and damage category considered, fertilization reduced damage by 18-44 per unit of cab- bage marketable yield (MY) in cases where plastic mulch (PM) was not applied. PM has in general favorable and statistical significant impact on yields; however, the benefits from PM are more pronounced in cases where fertilizers are not applied, and under certain fertilization schemes mulching had only a modest influence on yields. In fact, PM reduced by a third or even more than half the environmental gains achieved with fertilizations.

Based on the three-year average results it can be stated that PM is likely to reduce environmental impacts per unit of MY in 12 out of 18 midpoint categories considered by the ReCiPe 2016 method. The posi- tive environmental impact of PM application resulted from lower con- sumption of irrigation water, lower fuel (diesel) consumption, lower nitrogen emissions from soil and lower machinery use per unit of MY achieved; and negative impact was mainly the consequence of the pro- duction and end-of-life treatment of PM. In the three aggregated endpoint categories clear environmental benefits from PM were deter- mined only in the ecosystem quality category mainly due to reduced water and land requirements. Considering human health and resources availability indicators, the positive effect of mulching was determined only in the case of treatment without fertilization because of the highest difference in MY. Although on-site burning is one of the most common and technically most readily applied end-of-life PM treatment, it is also the most unacceptable solution from the environmental aspect. Land- filling or incineration of PM in MSWI with energy recovery would reduce the environmental impacts of cabbage production on human health by 16-21% respectively, as compared to the scenario which
assumes that PM is burned on-site. The experiment was conducted with

86: Under conditions set up in the experiment, fertilizing had positive and statistically significant effect on yields of spring cabbage. Regardless of the fertilization method applied, increased yields due to fertilization completely offset the increased damage caused by the application of fertilizers. Depending on the fertilization method and damage category considered, fertilization reduced damage by 18-44 per unit of cab- bage marketable yield (MY) in cases where plastic mulch (PM) was not applied. PM has in general favorable and statistical significant impact on yields; however, the benefits from PM are more pronounced in cases where fertilizers are not applied, and under certain fertilization schemes mulching had only a modest influence on yields. In fact, PM reduced by a third or even more than half the environmental gains achieved with fertilizations.

Based on the three-year average results it can be stated that PM is likely to reduce environmental impacts per unit of MY in 12 out of 18 midpoint categories considered by the ReCiPe 2016 method. The posi- tive environmental impact of PM application resulted from lower con- sumption of irrigation water, lower fuel (diesel) consumption, lower nitrogen emissions from soil and lower machinery use per unit of MY achieved; and negative impact was mainly the consequence of the pro- duction and end-of-life treatment of PM. In the three aggregated endpoint categories clear environmental benefits from PM were deter- mined only in the ecosystem quality category mainly due to reduced water and land requirements. Considering human health and resources availability indicators, the positive effect of mulching was determined only in the case of treatment without fertilization because of the highest difference in MY. Although on-site burning is one of the most common and technically most readily applied end-of-life PM treatment, it is also the most unacceptable solution from the environmental aspect. Land- filling or incineration of PM in MSWI with energy recovery would reduce the environmental impacts of cabbage production on human health by 16-21% respectively, as compared to the scenario which
assumes that PM is burned on-site. The experiment was conducted with

87: Under conditions set up in the experiment, fertilizing had positive and statistically significant effect on yields of spring cabbage. Regardless of the fertilization method applied, increased yields due to fertilization completely offset the increased damage caused by the application of fertilizers. Depending on the fertilization method and damage category considered, fertilization reduced damage by 18-44 per unit of cab- bage marketable yield (MY) in cases where plastic mulch (PM) was not applied. PM has in general favorable and statistical significant impact on yields; however, the benefits from PM are more pronounced in cases where fertilizers are not applied, and under certain fertilization schemes mulching had only a modest influence on yields. In fact, PM reduced by a third or even more than half the environmental gains achieved with fertilizations.

Based on the three-year average results it can be stated that PM is likely to reduce environmental impacts per unit of MY in 12 out of 18 midpoint categories considered by the ReCiPe 2016 method. The posi- tive environmental impact of PM application resulted from lower con- sumption of irrigation water, lower fuel (diesel) consumption, lower nitrogen emissions from soil and lower machinery use per unit of MY achieved; and negative impact was mainly the consequence of the pro- duction and end-of-life treatment of PM. In the three aggregated endpoint categories clear environmental benefits from PM were deter- mined only in the ecosystem quality category mainly due to reduced water and land requirements. Considering human health and resources availability indicators, the positive effect of mulching was determined only in the case of treatment without fertilization because of the highest difference in MY. Although on-site burning is one of the most common and technically most readily applied end-of-life PM treatment, it is also the most unacceptable solution from the environmental aspect. Land- filling or incineration of PM in MSWI with energy recovery would reduce the environmental impacts of cabbage production on human health by 16-21% respectively, as compared to the scenario which
assumes that PM is burned on-site. The experiment was conducted with

88: Under conditions set up in the experiment, fertilizing had positive and statistically significant effect on yields of spring cabbage. Regardless of the fertilization method applied, increased yields due to fertilization completely offset the increased damage caused by the application of fertilizers. Depending on the fertilization method and damage category considered, fertilization reduced damage by 18-44 per unit of cab- bage marketable yield (MY) in cases where plastic mulch (PM) was not applied. PM has in general favorable and statistical significant impact on yields; however, the benefits from PM are more pronounced in cases where fertilizers are not applied, and under certain fertilization schemes mulching had only a modest influence on yields. In fact, PM reduced by a third or even more than half the environmental gains achieved with fertilizations.

Based on the three-year average results it can be stated that PM is likely to reduce environmental impacts per unit of MY in 12 out of 18 midpoint categories considered by the ReCiPe 2016 method. The posi- tive environmental impact of PM application resulted from lower con- sumption of irrigation water, lower fuel (diesel) consumption, lower nitrogen emissions from soil and lower machinery use per unit of MY achieved; and negative impact was mainly the consequence of the pro- duction and end-of-life treatment of PM. In the three aggregated endpoint categories clear environmental benefits from PM were deter- mined only in the ecosystem quality category mainly due to reduced water and land requirements. Considering human health and resources availability indicators, the positive effect of mulching was determined only in the case of treatment without fertilization because of the highest difference in MY. Although on-site burning is one of the most common and technically most readily applied end-of-life PM treatment, it is also the most unacceptable solution from the environmental aspect. Land- filling or incineration of PM in MSWI with energy recovery would reduce the environmental impacts of cabbage production on human health by 16-21% respectively, as compared to the scenario which
assumes that PM is burned on-site. The experiment was conducted with

89: Under conditions set up in the experiment, fertilizing had positive and statistically significant effect on yields of spring cabbage. Regardless of the fertilization method applied, increased yields due to fertilization completely offset the increased damage caused by the application of fertilizers. Depending on the fertilization method and damage category considered, fertilization reduced damage by 18-44 per unit of cab- bage marketable yield (MY) in cases where plastic mulch (PM) was not applied. PM has in general favorable and statistical significant impact on yields; however, the benefits from PM are more pronounced in cases where fertilizers are not applied, and under certain fertilization schemes mulching had only a modest influence on yields. In fact, PM reduced by a third or even more than half the environmental gains achieved with fertilizations.

Based on the three-year average results it can be stated that PM is likely to reduce environmental impacts per unit of MY in 12 out of 18 midpoint categories considered by the ReCiPe 2016 method. The posi- tive environmental impact of PM application resulted from lower con- sumption of irrigation water, lower fuel (diesel) consumption, lower nitrogen emissions from soil and lower machinery use per unit of MY achieved; and negative impact was mainly the consequence of the pro- duction and end-of-life treatment of PM. In the three aggregated endpoint categories clear environmental benefits from PM were deter- mined only in the ecosystem quality category mainly due to reduced water and land requirements. Considering human health and resources availability indicators, the positive effect of mulching was determined only in the case of treatment without fertilization because of the highest difference in MY. Although on-site burning is one of the most common and technically most readily applied end-of-life PM treatment, it is also the most unacceptable solution from the environmental aspect. Land- filling or incineration of PM in MSWI with energy recovery would reduce the environmental impacts of cabbage production on human health by 16-21% respectively, as compared to the scenario which
assumes that PM is burned on-site. The experiment was conducted with
