90: Under conditions set up in the experiment, fertilizing had positive and statistically significant effect on yields of spring cabbage. Regardless of the fertilization method applied, increased yields due to fertilization completely offset the increased damage caused by the application of fertilizers. Depending on the fertilization method and damage category considered, fertilization reduced damage by 18-44 per unit of cab- bage marketable yield (MY) in cases where plastic mulch (PM) was not applied. PM has in general favorable and statistical significant impact on yields; however, the benefits from PM are more pronounced in cases where fertilizers are not applied, and under certain fertilization schemes mulching had only a modest influence on yields. In fact, PM reduced by a third or even more than half the environmental gains achieved with fertilizations.

Based on the three-year average results it can be stated that PM is likely to reduce environmental impacts per unit of MY in 12 out of 18 midpoint categories considered by the ReCiPe 2016 method. The posi- tive environmental impact of PM application resulted from lower con- sumption of irrigation water, lower fuel (diesel) consumption, lower nitrogen emissions from soil and lower machinery use per unit of MY achieved; and negative impact was mainly the consequence of the pro- duction and end-of-life treatment of PM. In the three aggregated endpoint categories clear environmental benefits from PM were deter- mined only in the ecosystem quality category mainly due to reduced water and land requirements. Considering human health and resources availability indicators, the positive effect of mulching was determined only in the case of treatment without fertilization because of the highest difference in MY. Although on-site burning is one of the most common and technically most readily applied end-of-life PM treatment, it is also the most unacceptable solution from the environmental aspect. Land- filling or incineration of PM in MSWI with energy recovery would reduce the environmental impacts of cabbage production on human health by 16-21% respectively, as compared to the scenario which
assumes that PM is burned on-site. The experiment was conducted with

91: Under conditions set up in the experiment, fertilizing had positive and statistically significant effect on yields of spring cabbage. Regardless of the fertilization method applied, increased yields due to fertilization completely offset the increased damage caused by the application of fertilizers. Depending on the fertilization method and damage category considered, fertilization reduced damage by 18-44 per unit of cab- bage marketable yield (MY) in cases where plastic mulch (PM) was not applied. PM has in general favorable and statistical significant impact on yields; however, the benefits from PM are more pronounced in cases where fertilizers are not applied, and under certain fertilization schemes mulching had only a modest influence on yields. In fact, PM reduced by a third or even more than half the environmental gains achieved with fertilizations.

Based on the three-year average results it can be stated that PM is likely to reduce environmental impacts per unit of MY in 12 out of 18 midpoint categories considered by the ReCiPe 2016 method. The posi- tive environmental impact of PM application resulted from lower con- sumption of irrigation water, lower fuel (diesel) consumption, lower nitrogen emissions from soil and lower machinery use per unit of MY achieved; and negative impact was mainly the consequence of the pro- duction and end-of-life treatment of PM. In the three aggregated endpoint categories clear environmental benefits from PM were deter- mined only in the ecosystem quality category mainly due to reduced water and land requirements. Considering human health and resources availability indicators, the positive effect of mulching was determined only in the case of treatment without fertilization because of the highest difference in MY. Although on-site burning is one of the most common and technically most readily applied end-of-life PM treatment, it is also the most unacceptable solution from the environmental aspect. Land- filling or incineration of PM in MSWI with energy recovery would reduce the environmental impacts of cabbage production on human health by 16-21% respectively, as compared to the scenario which
assumes that PM is burned on-site. The experiment was conducted with

92: Under conditions set up in the experiment, fertilizing had positive and statistically significant effect on yields of spring cabbage. Regardless of the fertilization method applied, increased yields due to fertilization completely offset the increased damage caused by the application of fertilizers. Depending on the fertilization method and damage category considered, fertilization reduced damage by 18-44 per unit of cab- bage marketable yield (MY) in cases where plastic mulch (PM) was not applied. PM has in general favorable and statistical significant impact on yields; however, the benefits from PM are more pronounced in cases where fertilizers are not applied, and under certain fertilization schemes mulching had only a modest influence on yields. In fact, PM reduced by a third or even more than half the environmental gains achieved with fertilizations.

Based on the three-year average results it can be stated that PM is likely to reduce environmental impacts per unit of MY in 12 out of 18 midpoint categories considered by the ReCiPe 2016 method. The posi- tive environmental impact of PM application resulted from lower con- sumption of irrigation water, lower fuel (diesel) consumption, lower nitrogen emissions from soil and lower machinery use per unit of MY achieved; and negative impact was mainly the consequence of the pro- duction and end-of-life treatment of PM. In the three aggregated endpoint categories clear environmental benefits from PM were deter- mined only in the ecosystem quality category mainly due to reduced water and land requirements. Considering human health and resources availability indicators, the positive effect of mulching was determined only in the case of treatment without fertilization because of the highest difference in MY. Although on-site burning is one of the most common and technically most readily applied end-of-life PM treatment, it is also the most unacceptable solution from the environmental aspect. Land- filling or incineration of PM in MSWI with energy recovery would reduce the environmental impacts of cabbage production on human health by 16-21% respectively, as compared to the scenario which
assumes that PM is burned on-site. The experiment was conducted with
